Notes:
Horizontal Conoco:
Can't communicate bellow gun 2 on surface tester. Went over with them the possibility of combo being in gun due 2.75” stems being the same size he said the combos are painted and it's not the issue. Also went over if det isn't connected won't have read past. He's going to swap out top three guns and see if that works.
Description of Incident: XC was notified that crews were having difficulty getting successful scans on multiple gunstrings.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: XC SQM was deployed to location to diagnose and assist. Upon arriving on location, guns were dissasembled. SQM observed that the internal charge tubes were compressed, resulting in the bulkhead connection to the upper stem was disconnected. Barrel and Tandem subs were checked to identofy and obstructions. Nothing foreign was present. It was noticed that the tandem subs were very rusty and large signs of corrosion.
Root Cause/Conclusion: The buildup of rust/corrosionon the internal bore of the tandem sub, would prevent the sealing surface of the Bullkhead and more importantly the Top End Plate to move with adequate friction into the bore. This friction would cause the entire charge tube to be seated further down into the barrel, while assembling would create additional force that the charge tube can handle.
Incident Summary: On May 26, 2026, a surface incident occurred involving Horizontal Wireline Services, affecting operations for ConocoPhillips. Crews experienced difficulty with gunstring scans. An investigation found compressed internal charge tubes and disconnected bulkhead connections, with the root cause determined to be rust and corrosion in the tandem subs preventing proper component seating.

Notes:

Scanned a gun with truck and it showed a short below gun 1 and wouldn't read the plug switch but everything read good with the surface tester. 

Hooked up another gun and it won't read past gun 6 and shows short circuit. They called me at this point and I had them install FW 189 and scanned but still won't read past gun 6 and showing a short circuit below gun 6. They are coming off the well and swapping again.
Description of Incident: Equalized ans opened well for stage 7. Before RIH switches were scanned and showed short at bottom of gun 1 and not reading the plug switch. Came off the well and picked up another gun. Stabbed back on, equalized, opened well and scan at 20ft in the hole showed short below gun 6. Engineer called XC rep while on the well and the Spartan logging panel was updated to Firmware 189. Switches were scanned again and still showing short below gun 6. Came back off and picked up a third gun and completed the stage.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Water entered in both gun 1 on the first attempt and water in gun 6 causing a ahort circuit.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Manufacturing machine shop defect. The keyway was machined all the way to the end of the barrel which allowed the water to enter the gun under the orings.
Incident Summary: A misrun incident occurred on May 25, 2026, for Liberty Energy Inc. (HighPeak Energy, LES-West Texas). The issue involved multiple gun failures due to short circuits caused by water ingress. The root cause was a product defect from a manufacturing machine shop error, where an incorrectly machined keyway allowed water to enter the guns.

Notes:
Go / Devon

Got a call from Travis, he lost communication with switches 5-7 after shooting gun 4. 

Was able to talk him through some stuff and regain connection. They then proceeded to shoot gun 5, 6, and 7(not shot on surface), before they lost communication again

Heading out there to pull logs and inspect what I can
Description of Incident: After firing Gun 4 on Stage 19 of the Forest 26-35 4H, GO Wireline experienced a short circuit, resulting in a loss of communication with the remaining switches in the toolstring. GO Wireline management contacted XConnect in an attempt to reestablish communication through software adjustments. Communication was successfully regained, allowing the crew to fire Guns 5 and 6 before communication was lost again. At that point, the PIC made the decision to pull out of hole. XConnect was immediately dispatched to location to begin the investigation.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: On Surface the spare barrel located at the top of the toolstring exhibited short circuit burn marks on the stem switch communication wire (white) port. The bottom end plate was removed and insulation testing with a megohmmeter indicated a leak path to ground. The mini bridge was then removed, confirming the leak was present along the beveled neck section of the insulator.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Investigation determined the loss of communication was caused by an electrical leak path to ground within the spare barrel located at the top of the toolstring. Inspection revealed short circuit burn marks at the stem switch communication wire (white) port, and further insulation testing confirmed the failure originated from the beveled neck section of the insulator.
Failed Component: XC Bridged Mini Bulkheads w/ Wire Harness ASM
Incident Summary: On May 17, 2026, a misrun incident occurred at the GOW-Williston site involving customer Go Wireline LLC and operating company Devon Energy. The incident, which was categorized as a product defect, involved a loss of communication with toolstring switches due to an electrical leak path to ground in a spare barrel.

Notes:
GO/Rockport/XC/Misfire

Engineer set the plug, shot four guns, and then lost communication to the det on gun 5. They tried the usual tricks with no luck, skipped the gun, and completed the stage. Once on surface they found that everything looked correct. The Molex was connected, there were no loose wires from the Molex, and the wires looked good. They installed a new switch and it read the det just fine. We will get the switch and inspect.

The engineer called back after making a successful run with the same type of misfire. He was able to set the plug, shoot five guns, recieved a "NO" on the detonator, and when he tried to shoot, he saw an open. When I got out there the crew had thrown the first switch away. They did have both guns separated and I was able to test a switch on both guns and read the detonator on the test box. I was also able to meter the detonator through the bottom pins. I did not see any other issue with the guns themselves. When I went through the log files for both runs I was able to confirm two different switches had been used for each run and I noticed a [CSN] error when both switches were being scanned when they returned a "NO" for the detonator. I sent the picture to the WhatsApp group and Chad and Hasan confirmed there had been a short. I took the switch they did have back to the shop. When I scanned the switch by itself it showed "NO" for the detonator but when I added a resistor to the switch it gave a "Chain Has Short Circuit" error. When I scanned the switch without a resistor, confirmed the switch, and then added the resistor; I was able to Arm the switch and saw an open when trying to fire which replicated what they saw on location.

The crew on Rockport is not running a DSP and I suspect they may have over volted both of the switches. Supposedly they had at least 12 runs on them. I will send this switch for an X-ray. @Robert Grissom will also overnight a DSP.

Notes:

Tier 1 had another g-force switch issue last night. Same problem as the other 2. Unable to read the switch below. @robert and myself went over the log files with Ethan at their shop. Incident Summary: On May 9, 2026, a misrun incident occurred at Tier 1 Completions Solutions, Inc., in the TIER-Oklahoma district, involving a recurring g-force switch issue that prevented the gun below the release tool from firing. The plan is to upgrade to a 950v Transistor.

Notes: Got a Call from GO wireline with Rockport. They had a misrun, while shooting Gun 8. System froze and reset itself. Wasn't able to shoot Gun 8 after that, then system said gun 9 no longer had a detonator.
Description of Incident: While firing Gun 8 on Stage 21 of the Sofia 2H, GO Wireline experienced a software communication interruption between the P2500 logging panel and the rack-mounted computer. This caused the system to freeze and required the gunstring to be rescanned. Once communication was reestablished, Gun 9 no longer indicated that a detonator was installed. The engineer attempted to fire the gun, but the attempt was unsuccessful. A decision was then made to skip Gun 9 and proceed with completing the stage. XConnect was subsequently dispatched to location to conduct a field investigation.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: The event logs within the Spartan Perforating Software for that stage showed a signal interruption between the P2500 logging panel and the rack-mounted computer at 20:58, at the same time the system was sending voltage to Gun 8 to initiate firing. Gun 9 was then inspected on surface. During initial testing, the surface test box was able to detect the detonator. However, further inspection revealed that the red wire had broken free from the Molex connection, resulting in intermittent communication.
Root Cause/Conclusion: The wiring configuration for the P2500 panel was inspected and verified for proper installation and secure connections. As a preventative measure, the USB Type-B cable was replaced to reduce the risk of any future communication issues. The XConnect SQM remained on location for the next three consecutive stages and observed no further issues. The detonator from Gun 9 was removed from service.
Incident Summary: On March 25, 2026, a perforating incident occurred during operations on Sofia 2H, Stage 21. A software communication interruption, experienced by GO Wireline, caused Gun 9 to fail to fire. The root cause was identified as a broken wire in Gun 9's Molex connection, leading to intermittent communication.

Root Cause/Conclusion: Bad Igniter from the Manufacturer, also could have been caught on surface before RIH.
Incident Summary: An operational incident occurred on March 23, 2026, where an igniter failed due to a short circuit, showing zero resistance and preventing a plug from being set. The root cause was identified as a manufacturing defect in the igniter, with a bridge wire shorting internally. The faulty igniter has been sent to the vendor for further investigation into the manufacturing process.

Notes: Had a misfire with GO last night with Phoenix. Couldn't get Gun 1 to shoot after plug set. They ended up skipping the gun and finishing the stage. Heading out there now to grab the switch and logs
Description of Incident: GO Wireline experienced a misfire on Phoenix during stage operations. After the plug was set, the crew made four attempts to fire Gun 1, and proper voltage and current were indicated during each firing attempt. Despite this, Gun 1 failed to function as intended. Following the unsuccessful attempts, the decision was made to bypass the gun and continue with the stage to complete the operation. GO Wireline set the gun aside for XConnect
Investigation and Troubleshooting: The combo switch registered the presence of a detonator even though no detonator had been installed. Upon disassembly of the carrier, damage was identified on the green wire where it had been rubbing against the edge of the ballast. The exposed conductor likely caused the false detonator indication by unintentionally completing the circuit. However, the wire was actually shorting to the carrier, preventing the detonator from receiving the current necessary for initiation.
Root Cause/Conclusion: The root cause was damage to the green wire caused by contact with the ballast edge inside the carrier, which exposed the conductor and allowed it to short against the carrier. This created a false detonator indication and prevented proper current delivery to the detonator, resulting in the misfire.
Incident Summary: On March 23, 2026, GO Wireline experienced a misfire during stage operations for Phoenix Operating LLC, where Gun 1 failed to function despite proper firing attempts. Investigation revealed a damaged green wire caused a false detonator indication and shorted, preventing the gun from initiating. The gun was bypassed and set aside for XConnect.

Description of Incident: GO Wireline reported a communication issue with Gun #10 after firing Gun #9. Management was notified, and the decision was made to skip that gun and continue with the rest of the gunstring. Gun #10 was then sent back to Williston for XConnect investigation.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Gun #10 was disassembled for inspection. Scotch locks were checked to confirm proper crimping, and grounding continuity was verified. The switch was removed from the carrier and tested independently with no issues found. All wiring was individually metered and visually inspected for any damage or deformation.
Root Cause/Conclusion: During metering of the wire assemblies, an intermittent connection was identified on the incoming communication wire (white). Further inspection revealed that the rail female insulator had a faulty internal crimp, likely damaged by the shock from the gun below firing.
Incident Summary: An incident occurred on March 17, 2026, where GO Wireline reported a communication issue with Gun #10. The gun was skipped and sent for investigation. Initial inspection involved disassembling Gun #10 and checking components, but no independent issues were found.

Notes: Open Circuit Detected Above BRT after firing plug and guns 1-5
Description of Incident: An open circuit was detected above the (BRT) and toolstring immediately following the successful deployment of the plug and firing of guns 1 through 5.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
The BRT Switch (Address 13F7) underwent a detailed visual teardown. The outer shrink tubing was removed to allow for a direct observation of the switchboard components and associated wiring. All internal circuitry, solder points, and leads appeared intact, with no visible signs of carbonization, fraying, or mechanical stress.

Following the visual inspection, the switch was connected to a surface tester for functional verification. The test was successful, showing a stable Node Voltage of 26V and an adequate Current of 1.6mA. These readings confirm that the switch is communicating correctly and operating within standard electrical specifications.

Prior shot plots were audited to identify any electrical anomalies, such as excessively high currents or voltage spikes, that could have preceded the open circuit. All recorded data remained within the safe operating range, ruling out the potential for MOSFET damage or board circuitry overheating. Furthermore, a review of the log files showed that every written prompt was within reason, with no indications of intermittent communication or system warnings that would suggest a pending failure.
Root Cause/Conclusion: The investigation confirms that Switch 13F7 is in full working order. Because the switch passed all surface diagnostics and showed no signs of thermal or electrical degradation, the open circuit detected downhole likely originated from a physical break or connection failure elsewhere in the toolstring—specifically between the BRT and the upper tool components—triggered by the mechanical shock of the Gun 5 detonation.
Incident Summary: On February 17, 2026, an open circuit was detected after the successful deployment and firing of guns. An investigation into the BRT Switch (Address 13F7) found it to be intact and functional, with no electrical anomalies or signs of failure detected in prior data.

In the top/spare gun there was leakage on the through wire and found ark marks as well as brass on the through wire bulkhead.
Description of Incident: The engineer was able to set the plug, shoot gun 1, shoot gun 2, and then received a short below the BRT switch. He was unable to address gun 3 or any gun below the BRT switch. After troubleshooting the decision was made to POOH.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
On surface we found a short from the through wire banana pin to the endplate on the top gun directly below the BRT and also observed burn marks around the same pin caused by arcing. 

After removing the mini bulkhead from the endplate a piece of brass was found on the outside of the bulkhead for the ground wire.
Root Cause/Conclusion: During the manufacturing process a small piece of brass was left on the outside of the bulkhead molding that caused a short while trying to address gun 3. The engineer was able to set the plug and shoot the first two guns due to the small size of the brass fragment. The piece of brass finally caused a short at gun three due to a buildup of capacitance while setting the plug and shooting two guns, and/or the bulkhead moving around in the endplate seat finally making contact with the endplate when addressing gun 3.
Incident Summary: An operational incident occurred on March 5, 2026. During gun operations, a short circuit was detected below the BRT switch, preventing further gun activation and leading to the decision to pull out of hole. The investigation found a short from a wire pin to the endplate on the top gun below the BRT, with burn marks from arcing, and a piece of brass near the ground wire.

Notes: Evidently liberty high peak had a miss run last night. They were unable to set the plug and had to pull out of the well. On surface, the igniter was only reading 17 ohms. They have set the igniter to side but are unsure if the switch was set aside as well. They are going to email the log file over
Description of Incident: During wireline operations, the crew was unable to set the plug after two distinct firing attempts. The toolstring was subsequently pulled out of the hole (POOH) for surface inspection. Upon surface teardown, the igniter had not fired and was reading an abnormal 17 ohms. Log files and shot plots were submitted for analysis, and surface testing was performed on the recovered components to determine if the failure was due to downhole equipment malfunction or operational procedures.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: 1. Event Summary: During wireline operations, the crew was unable to set the plug after two distinct firing attempts. The toolstring was subsequently pulled out of the hole (POOH) for surface inspection. Upon surface teardown, the igniter had not fired and was reading an abnormal 17 ohms. Log files and shot plots were submitted for analysis, and surface testing was performed on the recovered components to determine if the failure was due to downhole equipment malfunction or operational procedures. 2. Evidence & Analysis A. Log and Shot Plot Review A review of the logs and shot plots for the plug (Address 5283) showed two firing attempts: • Attempt 1: Current was applied for approximately 0.5 seconds, reaching ~1.2A.(Figure1) • Attempt 2: Current was applied for approximately 1.5 seconds, establishing a smooth plateau around 0.9A–1.2A.(Figure2) • Baseline Comparison: A review of successful plug sets from other runs shows that a normal sequence requires 3 to 5 seconds of sustained current. Additionally, successful shots display a jagged, erratic current plateau, which is the electrical signature of the bridgewire physically burning. • Finding: The plots from this event lacked the jagged burning phase. The smooth plateaus and sudden, vertical drop-offs indicate the electrical circuit was manually cut from the surface before the thermal reaction could initiate. B. Physical Evidence (Surface Teardown & Testing) • Initial Igniter State: The recovered igniter tested at 17 ohms on the surface. This confirms the bridgewire was subjected to extreme heat via current application downhole but never reached the flashpoint. • Switch Testing: The addressable switch was tested on the surface using the panels and functioned perfectly, successfully shooting resistors through it. • Igniter Firing Test: The recovered 17-ohm igniter was subsequently tested on the surface. When a complete firing sequence was applied, the igniter successfully fired. • Finding: The downhole hardware was 100% operational. The surface tests definitively rule out any mechanical or electrical failure of the switch or the igniter.
Root Cause/Conclusion: 3. Root Cause: The failure to set the plug was exclusively caused by operator error due to premature termination of the firing sequence. The operator released the firing buttons before the igniter had sufficient time to heat up and initiate the pyrotechnic train. The fact that the exact switch and igniter from the miss run functioned flawlessly during surface testing proves the downhole equipment was healthy but was simply not supplied with sustained power long enough to operate. 4. Recommendations & Corrective Actions • Operator Briefing: Review the differences between detonators (instantaneous) and igniters (thermal/time-dependent) with the wireline crew so they understand why the longer hold time is necessary. • Procedural Adherence (On-Screen Prompts): Operators must strictly follow the software's on-screen prompts for proper holding. The system actively displays an orange warning box during the sequence: "Keep pressing both fire buttons as long as you see this message or until current breaks."
Incident Summary: On March 4, 2026, an incident occurred during wireline operations where a plug could not be set after two firing attempts. Investigation revealed that the downhole equipment was fully operational. The root cause was determined to be that the electrical circuit was manually cut from the surface prematurely, preventing the igniter from firing.

Root Cause/Conclusion: Pending manufacture investigation.

Notes:
While shooting stage WL engineer was prompted with an open warning after shooting gun #7
bellow gun #9. Multiple attempts to reestablish communication were made without success. It
was decided to skip the gun and continue shooting the remaining guns.
Description of Incident:
While shooting stage WL engineer was prompted with an open warning after shooting gun #7
bellow gun #9. Multiple attempts to reestablish communication were made without success. It
was decided to skip the gun and continue shooting the remaining guns.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Once on surface gun and switch stem were checked successfully with surface tester. Gun and
switch stem were visible checked. No issues with wired connection were observed. Gun #8,
switch stem #8, gun #9, and switch stem #9 were sent back to XConnect for further analysis.

Gun #8 was removed and subjected to a detailed inspection. All associated wiring,
connectors, and terminal interfaces were examined and verified to be intact, properly
terminated, and electrically functional. No evidence of conductor damage, insulation
breach, or terminal back-out was observed.
During the mechanical
inspection, a significant
deformation (crush) of the
charge tube was identified.
Dimensional verification
confirmed an approximate
reduction in overall charge tube
length of ~3/16 inch relative to
specification. (see picture bellow)
This dimensional reduction was
sufficient to induce an
intermittent electrical
communication with all guns
positioned below Gun #8. The
deformation likely introduced
inconsistent contact pressure
between end plate and switch stem, leading to unstable signal continuity.

This condition was corroborated through
shooting software diagnostics during stage
execution and inventory. The system log
displayed multiple automatic rescans, visually
indicated by a blue line, signifying repeated
attempts to reestablish communication. These
intermittent communication recoveries persisted
until Gun #8 was skipped.
The findings support that the charge tube
deformation in Gun #8 was the primary
contributor to the intermittent and eventual loss
of communication to gun #8.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
The charge tube crush along with the corroboration from the software inventory of
the switches it is believed that the charge tube was crushed during field assembly.
It is unknown what exactly caused the charge tube crush, but factors that can
cause charge tube crush are as follows.
• Improper assembly techniques resulting in excessive compressive loading during
make-up
• Insufficient lubrication (grease) applied to mating components, leading to
increased friction and abnormal torque transfer
• Inadequate cleaning of subs prior to assembly
• Presence of debris, foreign material, or corrosion within the sub bore
• Use of rusted or dimensionally compromised subs
Any of the above conditions can result in improper load distribution, excessive axial
stress, or interference during make-up, ultimately leading to tube compression and
dimensional reduction.
To mitigate recurrence, an XConnect field technician will be dispatched to the location
to review proper assembly procedures with personnel. This will include reinforcement of
correct lubrication practices, sub inspection and cleaning requirements, torque
application guidance, and identification of conditions that may contribute to mechanical
deformation during build-up.

Notes: Surefire Wireline reported a misrun in which the toolstring did not properly arm the required number of switches once at depth. Subsequent troubleshooting identified **Gun 4, Switch ID E807** as defective. The suspected cause is molding material intrusion during the manufacturing process.
Description of Incident: Surefire Wireline reported a misrun in which the toolstring failed to properly arm the required number of switches while on surface. The crew replaced the suspected switch and then ran the toolstring downhole, performing periodic communication checks with no issues observed. However, once at depth, the gunstring was unable to properly read all switches.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Surefire Wireline provided the XFire switches from the misrun to XConnect personnel for evaluation. Initial shop testing did not identify any issues with the switches. XConnect subsequently dispatched to location and disassembled the gunstring one barrel at a time to inspect for abnormalities within the carriers. The gun barrels were then transported to the XConnect Williston shop for further analysis. The barrels and switches were mocked up in the same configuration used on location to replicate the conditions of the failure. XConnect successfully reproduced the issue when assembling Guns 4 through 1.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Troubleshooting confirmed **Gun 4, Switch ID E807** was defective. The switch intermittently reported an incorrect switch count downstream. This failure mode misleadingly indicated a fault in the next in-line switch; however, the root abnormality originated with the Gun 4 switch. The suspected cause is molding material intrusion during the manufacturing process. Once the switch was swapped out, The gunstring read without issues

Investigation and Troubleshooting: During the surface investigation, it was discovered that the ground wire for the Molex/detonator connection had been cut. See image 1 below. It appears that it was pinched between the bottom end plate and the carrier itself. See image 2. This could have occurred during the detonator installation when someone accidentally pulled on the wrong wire during the pull test. The operator overlooked the issue, and the charge tube was reinserted into the carrier. There was just enough connection made by the pinched ground wire to indicate that the detonator had been installed. Unfortunately, after the plug and gun 1 were shot, the wire was completely broken. The test would not have been successful with the test box on the surface if the wire had been severed before running in the well.
Root Cause/Conclusion: This could have occurred during the detonator installation when someone accidentally pulled on the wrong wire during the pull test. The operator overlooked the issue, and the charge tube was reinserted into the carrier.

Notes:
GO engineer RIH to approximately 9,000 feet (top of liner) and while waiting for water transfer, he inventoried his switches. He was receiving intermittent communication, and the decision was made to POOH.
Once on surface the crew discovered the 2nd gun from bottom was flooded and both O-rings on the top of the gun were broken and appeared to be burnt.
The gun was set aside for XConnect to investigate further.
Description of Incident:
GO engineer RIH to approximately 9,000 feet (top of liner) and while waiting for water transfer, he inventoried his switches. He was receiving intermittent communication, and the decision was made to POOH.
Once on surface the crew discovered the 2nd gun from bottom was flooded and both O-rings on the top of the gun were broken and appeared to be burnt.
The gun was set aside for XConnect to investigate further.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Each gun has a tandem sub with 4 ea. 230 O-rings (2 on each side) installed on the top of the barrel and a green protective cap covering the exposed O-rings during assembly. The O-rings are black and coated with green paint. On location the green cap is removed, the two topside O-rings are greased, and the barrel is threaded into the next gun in line. 
The two O-rings that failed were on the top side of the tandem sub of gun 1 which caused gun 2 to flood. Upon closer inspection the green paint was rubbed off reveling the black O-ring but did not burn. It appears that at some point the two O-rings had rolled over, either during the installation process or when the green cap was removed. Subsequently when the sub was threaded into the next barrel the O-rings were cut resulting in the flooded barrel.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
1.	XConnect will review the O-Ring and protective cap installation process and QC verification process for any gaps.

2.	XConnect also recommends visually checking top side O-Rings before threading gun barrels together in the field.

Notes: Broken Banana Pin
Description of Incident: GO Wireline was having multiple broken banana pins on the top side of the tandem subs
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
An XConnect representative went to location to troubleshoot and did not notice any issues with the gun building process. When the Rail carrier was placed on a flat surface, he discovered that one ballast sat higher than the other ballast. He was able to show this by measuring the height from the flat surface to the middle of the bulkhead terminal. This slight angle in the bottom ballast was causing the pin to contact the bulkhead, rather than the terminal, which resulted in the banana pin breaking when the gun was made up to the next gun.
It was determined that there were two different ballasts installed. The new ballast has a slightly larger OD for better centralization (by ~0.025’’) within the barrel. When the new ballast is installed on the top of the carrier and the smaller (old version) is installed on the bottom of the carrier (switch side) causes the bulkhead to contact the banana pin and break them.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
1.	For the guns on location the decision was made to rotate the carrier 180 degrees which allowed the carrier to sit level, removing the angle of the ballast from the equation. 
2.	Moving forward XConnect will install the old ballast on the top side of the carrier and the new ballast on the switch side of the carrier until the old ballast inventory is used.

Upon arrival, XConnect SQM disassembled the barrel in question for inspection. The shaped charge carrier was found to be free of defects. Description of Incident: XConnect was called because the engineer was receiving a short after gun 8. He was able to set the plug and shoot gun 1 before the switch showed a short. The engineer was able to read the switch with the surface test box through the slip rings, so the surface panels were swapped out to make sure there was not a short at the panel. The issue persisted and the decision was made to skip gun 2 through 7 and shoot guns 8 through 11.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Once on surface the shot guns were removed and the tool string verified again with the verifier box. The switch stem for gun 7 was removed from the tool string and when tested with the verifier box independently it showed “yes” for a detonator which indicated a short.
While troubleshooting the gun system the SQM noticed a lot of moisture on the bulkhead of gun 7, on other switch stems in the trailer, as well as in the sub cart used when breaking down guns. The concern about excessive moisture was communicated with the crew and they understood saying they had been cleaning the switch stems before installing them on new guns.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
The switch stem was returned to the XConnect facility and further evidence of moisture and corrosion was discovered. 
The moisture was able to travel into the switch molding and reach the switch board creating corrosion that eventually caused the stem to short out.

Notes: Gun 1 split open due to setting tool pressure event
Description of Incident: Set plug with very little indication of plug set. Couldn't read gun 1 and was showing a short after setting plug. Gun 1 was skipped, guns 2-18 were shot and frac continued as planned.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Once OOH, Gun 1 had split just above the firing head and was extremely swollen at the top of the barrel as well due to the pressure from the setting tool migrating up into the barrel of gun 1 forcing the charge tube and charges toward the top of the barrel. The setting tool was still fully stoked. The setting tool and gun were left together and taken back to the Nine Energy Shop
Root Cause/Conclusion: Have a meeting with XC management to discuss sending the setting tool off to a 3rd party vendor who will be unbiased and highly specialized to inspect the setting tool for the exact route cause of the excessive pressure event in the top power charge chamber portion of the setting tool.

Notes: Gun 1 split due to setting tool pressure event
Description of Incident: Set plug with little indication of plug set. After setting the plug, Gun 1 would not read and showed a short circuit. Gun 1 was skipped and guns 2-18 were shot.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Once OOH, Gun 1 had split in 2 places due to excessive pressure migration from the Baker 20 Setting tool.  The setting tool was not stroked out and had stroked back in due to the pressure bleeding off from the open holes in the barrel. The setting tool and Gun 1 were taken back to the Nine Energy shop to investigate in a controlled environment. All the Orings were in place on the setting tool, the bleeder nut was tight, the oil was at the proper level. The allen screw for the collar that retains the crosslink key was broken.  No part of the igniter was found in XC Baker 20 firing head. The igniter and igniter bulkhead more than likely shot up into the bottom of gun 1 then came out the bottom ruptured part of the barrel. The bottom bulkhead in Gun 1 was forced several inched towards the top of gun 1. No portions of the XC molded switch stem were found in the bottom of gun 1. Parts of the molded switch stem were found in the power charge chamber as well as a hardened piece of power charge that was shaped like a railroad spike. Below the power charge chamber there was some more ruminants of the switch stem and one ground plunger from the switch stem. It looked like the piece of power charge that was shaped like a railroad spike tried to go through the bottom of the power charge chamber and blocked the gas from getting to the floating piston. This restriction in the bottom hole of the power charge chamber could have possibly caused the pressure in the power charge chamber to increase.
Root Cause/Conclusion: The power charged and igniters are being brought from location for testing. The hardened piece on railroad spike looking power charge is going to be sent off and analyzed.

Description of Incident: A similar communication failure like event 459 and 460 occurred in a subsequent stage, with GO Wireline again reporting loss of communication following gun firing. An XConnect SQM was dispatched to investigate.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: During inspection, it was discovered that GO Wireline had swapped out wireline units at some point prior to these events. The replacement unit was operating with a Probe Shooting Panel instead of the required Digital Shooting Panel. The Probe Shooting Panel is known to cause overcurrent conditions that damage AWS switches during firing sequences.
Root Cause/Conclusion: To resolve the issue, the Probe Shooting Panel was removed and replaced with the proper Digital Shooting Panel on the affected wireline unit. All Xfire AWS switches that had been exposed to the Probe Panel were pulled from service and replaced.
Failed Component: XC XFire Stem - FAST Single ASM

Notes: GO Wireline lost communication with the bottom gun and plug while RIH, decision was made to POOH and swap guns. While POOH, gun reestablished connection. Gunstring was set aside for XC Investigation.
Description of Incident: GO Wireline reported an open-circuit communication issue during the initial communication check while running in hole. The combo switch (Gun 1 and Plug) could not be read, and the decision was made to pull out of hole (POOH). Upon coming out, full communication was re-established; however, the gunstring was still pulled to surface and set aside for XConnect Service Quality Manager (SQM) investigation.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: The XC SQM fully disassembled Guns 1 and 2. All wiring was found to be in good condition, and all components metered within acceptable parameters. The root cause was identified as an improper ground between the stem of Gun 2 and its respective tandem sub. The ground contact surface inside the tandem sub was packed with blast debris and corrosion, which caused intermittent communication. This also explains why communication was re-established by the downward force of the gunstring while pulling out of hole. Xconnect SQM asked GO Wireline field personnel how often they clean the ground surfaces of the tandem subs and determined that it was not done frequently enough.
Root Cause/Conclusion: The XConnect SQM communicated to GO Wireline management and field personnel the importance of maintaining a clean contact surface to ensure proper continuity within the gunstring. They emphasizing that the ground contact surface within the tandem sub should be cleaned after every stage using a wire brush wheel to maintain reliable communication.

Notes: GO Wireline called about a missrun the night prior. received short circuit during gun firing process
Description of Incident: GO Wireline reported a communication failure during a stage. After firing Gun 8, communication was lost with Guns 9 and 10. The GO Wireline Engineer attempted troubleshooting by sending positive voltage, which temporarily re-established communication with Gun 9. Gun 9 was successfully fired, but communication with Gun 10 was immediately lost thereafter. An XConnect Service Quality Manager (SQM) was dispatched to inspect the issue the following
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
GO Wireline personnel had identified a faulty stem switch as the suspected cause. The switch read correctly when tested individually, but during a deliberate short test it failed to display a switch address. Other switches identified properly before shorting. Further metering confirmed a leak between the ground port and outgoing signal (blue wire) and a dead short between incoming (white wire) and outgoing (blue) ports, whereas good switches measured ~11.45 MΩ

it was discovered that GO Wireline had swapped wireline units at some point and was using a Probe Shooting Panel instead of the recommended Digital Shooting Panel. The Probe Panel is known to create overcurrent conditions that damage AWS switches, and this was determined to be the ultimate root cause of all three incidents.
Root Cause/Conclusion: the Probe Shooting Panel was removed and replaced with the proper Digital Shooting Panel on the affected wireline unit. All Xfire AWS switches that had been exposed to the Probe Panel were pulled from service and replaced.
Failed Component: XC XFire Stem - FAST Single ASM

Notes:

Set plug, Shot 6 Guns, Received Short Circuit.
Description of Incident: Go Wireline reported a communication failure during a stage. After firing Gun 6, communication was lost with Guns 7, 8, and 9 out of a 10-gun string. An XConnect Service Quality Manager was immediately dispatched to conduct an inspection.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Upon arrival, XConnect SQM inspected Barrels 7 through 10 in detail. Guns 7, 8, and 10 showed no issues during testing. Gun 9 was seen to be the cause of the problem: the stem switch read correctly when tested alone, but when purposely shorted during testing, it failed to display a switch address prior to short circuit. In comparison, the other switches properly identified the switch address prior to being shorted. Further metering of the faulty stem against a new one revealed a small leak between the ground port and the outgoing signal (blue wire). Resistance readings also showed an issue, showing a dead short between Incoming signal port (White wire) and outgoing signal port (Blue Wire), whereas known good switches measured approximately 11.45MΩ.

it was discovered that GO Wireline had swapped wireline units at some point and was using a Probe Shooting Panel instead of the recommended Digital Shooting Panel. The Probe Panel is known to create overcurrent conditions that damage AWS switches, and this was determined to be the ultimate root cause of all three incidents.
Root Cause/Conclusion: the Probe Shooting Panel was removed and replaced with the proper Digital Shooting Panel on the affected wireline unit. All Xfire AWS switches that had been exposed to the Probe Panel were pulled from service and replaced.
Failed Component: XC XFire Stem - FAST Single ASM

Description of Incident:
Liberty Energy reported communication issues with three XConnect RAIL perforating barrels that
would not interface with the XC surface testing box. Liberty Wireline representatives isolated the
affected barrels and set them aside for inspection by the XConnect Service Quality Manager
(SQM).
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Upon arrival, the XConnect SQM inspected the three reported barrels. Each associated carrier
and tandem sub was broken down and visually examined for defects, and all wiring was metered
to confirm proper continuity.
The investigation determined that only one carrier was at fault. The Addressable XFire switch
within that carrier was able to receive a signal, but failed to transmit an outgoing communication
signal, indicating an internal failure of the switch.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
The faulty switch was removed and replaced. All three carriers were then reassembled and
successfully tested both individually and inline. Each demonstrated proper communication
functionality. Liberty Wireline personnel observed the repair and verified the retesting results.
Failed Component: RL XFire FAST Switch - Wired Harness 3/5 ASM

Notes: The engineer lost communication below gun one after setting the plug
Description of Incident: The supervisor ran in hole (RIH), set the plug, and initiated the perforating sequence. Following the detonation of gun 1, communication was lost with all guns positioned below the top gun. Despite attempts, communication could not be restored, so the tool string was held in position while the Company Man and Vertice worked to open the flapper on the plug. After approximately one hour of unsuccessful efforts, the decision was made to pull out of hole (POOH). Upon commencing POOH operations, the supervisor regained communication with all switches. The string was pumped back down, and operations resumed. An additional four guns were fired, bringing the total to five, after which communication was again lost below the top gun. Consequently, the tool string was pulled out to allow for the deployment of a new string and the completion of the stage.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Once the items were brought to the surface, they were set aside for further examination. The supervisor's investigation did not yield any definitive findings. All components were labeled and will be sent to the shop for additional analysis. No Xconnect representative was present when the gun string was dismantled at surface, as they had not been contacted during the incident.
Root Cause/Conclusion: The switch in question for this event will be sent to the Midland facility by a Tier 1 employee for further investigation.

After looking at the shot logs, the voltage was high and concerning. They are using probe panels. GO was thinking it was a software issue. I explained to Matt that they have been running the same version for a couple months now with no issues. Plan of action for now is to have the engineers watch the shot plot and not allow the voltage to get over 350v and get DSPs here to swap out their probe panels. @austin @ron can y’all send the DSPs you have up here ASAP. I am sure GO will be thinking they have been using Probe panels on Phoenix for a while with the same engineers and no issues until now so something may have changed in the switch that is more fragile. We can also ease their mind and confirm it isn’t a software issue if the problem occurs again we can revert back to 22.3.1 at the time of the problem and see if that version also won’t read the switch before skipping. @austin this sounds kind of like the same thing Brock was saying happened on Devon. They didn’t read and when he got OOH everything reads with verifier box and he couldn’t find the issue. Did you get them switches back?
Description of Incident: GO had two separate Incidents regarding a failure in full gun inventoring. Both resulting in Misfires.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Preliminary findings show a high application of voltage. Resulting in a failure in one of the Switches, thus not allowing feed-thru communication to a switch/switches below.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Iniital fixes would be to instruct the limit of voltage applied to each firing sequence. ~350v, visualized on the Spartan Shot Plot, Ensure calibration of Veriac shooting panels betweeen analog gauge and meter, provide Digital Shooting Panels to Customer.

Notes: Misfire on TWG Chevron. Pinched white wire on Combo gun. They were able to shoot the plug, but the force from that finished off the wire and shorted gun 1. They shot the rest of the guns and called it a stage. I am writing up a little report now to send to them, because Chevron will want one.
Description of Incident:
•	WL engineer got to depth and set plug.
•	After plug set, gun 1 was no longer communicating. 
•	WL Engineer could not get gun 1 to come back online.
•	Decision was made to Shoot remaining guns and call it a stage.
•	Once on surface WL Engineer found pinched white wire between det cover and ballast.
•	Determined to be shorted through wire on bottom Combo gun
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
o	Wire was routed between the det cover and the ballast body
o	Plug setting force caused the wire to go short against the zinc ballast.
o	Shorted through wire caused a loss of communication to the bottom switch.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
o	Xconnect addressed the issue with the assembly team and reinforced proper wire routing procedures and QC steps to prevent further pinched wires
o	Increase regular training intervals with Assembly staff to enforce proper wiring procedures.

Notes:
*GO Civitas – XC*
 *Update on DC Insert Breakage*

This is a follow-up on the earlier reports of DC Inserts breaking. I visited location to better understand what the crews were seeing and to observe both the installation and cleanup process. They mentioned that *eight inserts had broken within the past 36 hours*, all involving the same two DCs and the same two retainer nuts that have been in service for some time.
I grabbed a recently rebuilt DC tremoved its retainer nut and insert. The Retainer Nut in my opinion was heavily torqued. After disassembly, I found the insert had also cracked.

*Initial Measurements*

*Retainer Nut Measurements*
(_See the attached Print Screenshot_)
• A. Internal Depth: 4.014”
• B. Overall Length: 4.125”
• C. Top of Thread to Main Shoulder: 0.627”
• D. Main Body Length: 3.498”
*Insert Measurements (XBH500-203)*
*13 samples* were measured at the site, and *13 samples* at the GO Facility.

*On-Site Samples:*
• 2 brand new inserts measured at *4.597” and 4.598”*
• 1 previously shaved-down insert, likely from the earlier Gen 1 retainer nut issue
• 10 used or broken inserts measured between *4.587” and 4.592”* (after compression)
*GO Facility Samples:*
• 3 shaved-down inserts measured between *4.567”–4.573”*
• 10 new inserts measured consistently at *4.597”*
*Key Observations*
• All three retainer nuts (two on-site, one at the facility) showed signs of wear and potential over-torquing, specifically, flared outer shoulders and worn internal shoulders.
• Retainer nut main body lengths were slightly below spec:
    ◦ Facility sample: *3.495”*
    ◦ On-site: *3.497”–3.498”*
• Insert lengths were a bit over tolerance:
    ◦ Print spec: *4.575”  Tolerances are set at ± 0.015”*
    ◦ Several inserts measured around *4.597”*, putting them *0.007”–0.008” over* the upper limit of the Tolerance. .020-.023 over Print Diagram.
*Summary*
The combination of slightly oversized inserts and worn retainer nuts appears to the cause of these Inserts cracking
Horizontal Conoco is having another issue with getting an open to det.
Description of Incident: While shooting a gun the WL engineer saw an adequate detonator break, but the software still found the switch. The gun was skipped, and the remaining guns in the string were fired. Once the gun string was returned to surface it was found that a low order occurred. The det was fired but did not properly transfer to the det cord.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: An XConnect representative arrived on location 6/24/25 to investigate all the events for further clarification. While on location and speaking to the Horizontal crews; they explained that they had noticed a few Rail carriers having damaged detonator clips after being unloaded from the pallets. It is believed that this had occurred during the charge loading process and the perf design being 2/180’s. With a short rail carrier and the fact that the charges are 180 phasing, the broken clip occurred when the gun loader rolled the carrier to load the bottom charge, resulting in the detonator clip resting on the rail stand. While the Horizontal crews were able to catch some of the broken clips, it seems that the low order guns may have damaged clips (not visible to the crews on location) and once the detonator was loaded into the gun, the clip broke while RIH.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Changed the loading process and stand set up for 2/180 Rail carrier.

Notes: Plug still online when they got OOH with stage 44
Description of Incident: Once the tool was back to surface after shooting all the guns on stage 44, the setting tool had not stroked, and the plug was still attached. Continental pumped diverter balls to plug up the previous stage before they continued to treat stage 44.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Pintail FSM and XConnect representative went to location to investigate. The power charge was present and dry.  The igniter had not initiated and had a low ohm reading when checked with a blaster meter and was reading 35 ohms. The time log, shot plot, and event log were reviewed on the Spartan software. The time plot showed only gradual weight loss from the tool sliding due to well incline. The event log showed the igniter read 51ohms before attempting to shoot the igniter and read 35 ohms after. The shot plot showed that voltage and current were still coming up with no break and was held for 1.2 seconds. It appears that there wasn't enough current applied to the igniter to get it shoot and only partially burn to the resistor.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
Reiterate that the shooting panel buttons need to be held as prompted on the shot plot screen.
Discuss using a Cobra power charge that has the plug switch in the power charge that would be destroyed when the power charge burns so the customer knows if the plug switch still reads then the tool did not stroke.

Notes: Gun 9 didn’t shoot
Description of Incident: The engineer set the plug and shot guns 1-8. When he attempted to shoot gun 9 he had an open circuit while shooting getting voltage and no current on the shooting panel. He attempted a second time to shoot while applying positive voltage and seen a little current and assumed that gun 9 had shot then shot gun 10. Once he was OOH gun 9 did not fire
Investigation and Troubleshooting: This was and Xfire single molded fast switch. The node voltage reading of the switch on the surface tester in no reading 1V instead of 26V. This is a switch issue.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
Went over with the guys on location what to look for on the surface tester and in the truck showing them that the surface tester should show around 26V on the node voltage reading and not 1V or NA and in the truck the node voltage reading should be anywhere from 42 to 47. Explained to them that any node voltage reading that is low, the switch will still read the address and det but will have an open circuit when trying to fire downhole.
Switch Stem will be melted down to access the switch for further investigation to determine if there is an actual switch issue that needs to be fixed by the manufacturer or if the switch is being damaged during the molding process. Also had Standard switch stems overnighted to the Williston shop, and we will pull all the fast switches off location and replace them with standard switches as soon as they arrive.

Misfire after shooting 2 guns and plug. Flooded Barrel due to failed RL Bulkhead/lost retrainer nut
Description of Incident: Liberty Energy Services experienced a misfire due to a short circuit in Stage #19, Gun #3. The remainder of the toolstring functioned as expected and fired successfully.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: The toolstring was disassembled for inspection, revealing that Gun #3 had been compromised by well fluid ingress. Upon disassembly of the tandem subs, the O-rings were inspected and found to be in acceptable condition. The internal RL Bulkhead—responsible for electrical connection and internal sealing—was removed from the tandem sub for further examination. Although the retainer nut securing the Bulkhead against top side pressure was not visibly loose, it lacked sufficient tightness. Investigation determined that wellhead pressure had breached the bulkhead, creating a path for fluid to enter the upper gun. Both the molded material and O-rings along this pathway were found to be damaged.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Ensure Proper torque setting on retainer nut installation. Verify accuracy and efficiency of installation and Quality Control Processes.

Notes:
Screws over torqued and breaking. Zavanna/GO wireline
Caught at there shop.
Description of Incident: GO wireline called because they found a couple of Rail assemblies where the screw broke off from the ballast.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
1.	The first Rail showed signs that the screw may have been over torqued. The screw on the opposite side of the ballast was also stripped. The Rail SOP was referenced and the wording during the tightening of the screws to the ballast states to “Go back and fully tighten” and does not address the proper torque setting on the impact wrench to be used. 
2.	During the discussion at XConnect about this incident, it was communicated that there was a new employee working on the ballasts at the time of assembly, but there was no way to determine if the wrong torque setting was used or if they had been the individual who tightened the screw in question.
3.	There is also a concern that this batch of screws could be affected by Hydrogen Embrittlement, which is seen in high strength metals, and is cause by the absorption of hydrogen atoms. If this occurs the metal loses ductility and can fail at lower stresses than the expected yield strength.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
Currently the exact cause of the screw breaking is not clear. It could be due to an individual using the incorrect torque settings or due to Hydrogen Embrittlement. XConnect will test other screws to see if any fail under stress while using the correct torque setting. 

It is suggested that the SOP should be updated to state which correct torque setting should be used during assembly.

Notes: Had to skip a gun again. He tried twice and both times the detonator showed open.
Description of Incident: When trying to shoot gun 3 the engineer saw an open to the deto. The next scan said no deto present. His second attempt at shooting it showed an open as well. He skipped that gun and shot the spare.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Once I was on location the subs were still intact to both ends of the misfired barrel. I tried cleaning and reading through the top bulkhead, but it was damaged in a manner that it was shorted. 
I then took the carrier out of the barrel and read through it with the test box. It showed a deto present. I wiggle the molex connector and it showed no deto. 
While sliding the molex connector out I could see the green wire falling out. Once I removed the molex connector the green wire fell out. The tabs on the wire connector that slide into the molex connection were not bent or damaged. This tells me the wire was never fully seated into the molex connector.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
We reinspected the molex connector in ~350 guns on location. The reaming will be brought back to the shop for a pull test on the molex connectors.

Retrain assembly workers the proper way to pull test these wires.

Notes: They had a deto they could not get to go after 2 attempts they decided to skip it.
Description of Incident: The had a deto they could not get to go and ended up skipping the gun.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
I brought this barrel back to the shop for investigation. I have not been able to find anything wrong. I've shot through the switch several times. It will burn a resistor. I've manipulated the the red and green wire coming from the switch without any failures.
The problem had to be between the deto and the switch. The deto metered good when it was out of the hole.
This leaves:
 Bad wire crimp on the detos click in terminal
A broken wire making intermittent connection
Click in terminal was not pushed together all the way
Wire terminals were not fully seated into the click in terminal
An intermittent connection on the leg wires inside the det
A random issue with the det itself
I do not have the detonator or other half of the click in terminal to examine. It was put into another gun and sent down hole by night crew to be disposed of.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Reinspect the molex connector in the guns built for this job. Retrain assembly workers to pull test one wire at a time.

Notes: Lost communication to gun 7 after shooting gun 6. On surface they found gun 7 had flooded.
Description of Incident: After shooting gun 6 they lost communication to gun 7. Once on surface it was found that gun 7 had flooded.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
The blow by that flooded gun 7 when gun 6 was shot came through the bulkhead. Remanence of both o'rings are present and the castle nut was tight. I believe the integrity of the bulkhead was compromised prior to the shooting of gun 6.

The bulkhead could have been damaged during manufacturing, shipping, installation, or a combination of them.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
Tri-V has been notified and mentioned that sometimes a bulkhead sticks into the mold and they have to pop it out. This could have compromised the molding. They are going to take a closer look next time this happens.

Jeremy spoke with our assembly team. They are already putting hands on every bulkhead in order to grease the o'rings. From now on they will be performing a visual inspection as well.

Notes:
Liberty Oxy RAIL

While setting the plug current only increased for a split second and dropped to baseline. No indication of plug setting. Went to inventory switches and could not get past gun 3. Open Circuit.  Attempted Fix Wiring and manipulated tool string to regain communication with no success. POOH, and on my way out there.
Description of Incident: While Liberty Supervisor went to set the plug, it was observed that there was a sudden loss of current. The Supervisor then waited for normal sequence of plug setting indications, none of which occured. He went ahead and attempted to re-fire the Igniter but could not inventory the bottom 3 switches. The system prompted an open cicuit at every attempt. Fix Wiring mode was enabled, with the same result. The decision was made to POOH.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Upon reaching surface the gunstring was laid down and connected to the Surface Tester. The Surface Tester inidicated an open cicuit after 8 Switches(gun 3). Matching with what the supervisor had observed. The toolstirng was disconnected at the point of failure for examination. The Surface tester was connected to that section, resulting in a successful inventory of the switches. The tandem sub was inspected and deemed in working order. The uphole internals were pulled out of the carrier for physical inspection, all deemed adequate. A surface test was done to the upper section, while shorting out the feed thru of those internals, the surface tester did not pick up the short. It was then deemed to be an issue with the switch, or the feed-thru after the switch. I then cut the feed-thru in between the switch and RL Terminal to test both ends. While stripping the insulation off of the wire  the entire section of wire pulled out of the RL Terminal. Indicating an inadequate strip and/or crimp of the RL Female Terminal. The switch still attached to the gun was wired with an additional switch for verfiication of functionality. That portion passed the verification, verifying that the RL Female Terminal was the issue. In observation of the wire removed from the terminal, it is noticed that there is no exposed wire, just insulation. It is belived that during manufacturing that there is an error in the stripping process of the operation. Wire gauge was reduced due to broken strands, allowing for an inadequate crimp into the terminal. This also coincides with how the operation failed. Only a select amount of strands were connected, allowing for a series of inventory verifications. Upon applying shooting voltage, the lack of capacitance would degrade those few remaining strands, resulting in an open circuit.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Discuss with manufacturing facility on SOP's, Calibration of equipment, QC Checks.

Notes: The FSM for Caliber/SM called around 10:30pm and said they were pulling out of the well due to not being able to read past gun 2 at the marker joint. Notes: Couldn’t set plug. Short at bottom of BRT
Description of Incident: RIH with release tool 8 guns and plug. Reached target depth of 15266 and showed a short at the bottom of the brt. Moved gun around and got all switches back but was unable to fire. POOH with plug.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: The retainer nut on the attenuator sub was backed completely off and shorting over from the contact pin to the body.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
Short term:
Crews will take the attenuator sub off each run and make sure the retainer nut is tight, change the 222 orings, meg, and check resistance. We will start using Loctite thread tape around the threads of the nut to help with it not loosening up as well as monitoring it each run to see how the thread tape is doing. 

Long term:
Talk to our R &D team about making a 1-5/8 direct connect top sub for the XC gun system to eliminate using this attenuator sub or the need for a 1-7/8 to 1-5/8 crossover.
