Description of Incident: Caliber CLR had a misrun with an open after the BRT switch. They checked switches multiple times RIH. On bottom they were never able to arm the plug. Each time they tried reading switches they had the same open after the BRT switch. On surface after laying down they were able to read all switches.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: The FSM kept his day engineer on and broke down and rebuilt the BRT before I could get out here. The FSM assures me that the banana pin was tight. I was able to read the same BRT switch and a second switch through the BRT dart while knocking it around and tugging on wires trying to loose connection. I checked the female nut on the bottom sub of the BRT and the feed through pin makes good contact. I spoke with Ken at Titan and he will try and get schematics of the bottom sub of the BRT to help illustrate our feed through is making good contact.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
Swapping out the attenuating sub on the BRT, verifying internal lengths on the XConnect crossover and internal attenuating sub.

Engineering has determined that the contact pin on the universal quick change is not making consistent contact with the bottom nut on the attenuating sub.

Notes: Caliber-SM called at 11:50pm. The system was detecting an open below the BRT at 400ft. Tool string had been setting in the lube since 4pm. I am out here now waiting on their FSM to arrive and troubleshoot. The gun string still shows an open below the BRT on surface with the test box.
Description of Incident: Caliber-SM called at 11:50pm. The system was detecting an open below the BRT at 400ft. Tool string had been setting in the lube since 4pm. Pooh.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Gun string still shows open below BRT on surface. Took the off BRT and cross over and the gun string checked good with just the top sub on. (BRT and crossover were not broken apart. Then connected the BRT and crossover to a single gun, with top sub and it would only show one switch on the verifier box. Separated the crossover from release tool and single switch was found. Took the attenuator sub off the release tool and screwed the crossover straight into the bottom section of the BRT (one gun on still). Both switches were found. Took that apart and put the attenuator sub back on the BRT and checked it all together again still only found one switch. We then broke off the attenuator sub and crossover and metered both. No issues found. The FSM for Caliber rebuilt the attenuator sub and the insert for the crossover was replaced. Put everything back together and still could only detect one switch. We then separated the attenuator sub and crossover. Took off the nut holding the insert in the crossover and put it all back together. Two switches were found. Took it back apart put the nut back on and we were not able to replicate the issue again. Two switches could always be detected. There was possibly some kind of connection issue between the crossover and attenuator sub but we were not able to get it to fail again.
Root Cause/Conclusion: BRT and crossover were taken out of service for further inspection

Notes: Liberty OXY RAIL. Had another Bulkhead failure. Thankfully it did not cause a misrun, just trapped pressure on the PSA.
Description of Incident: Trapped pressure was observed at the PSA
Investigation and Troubleshooting: RL Bulkhead was cracked, releasing well fluid into the PSA
Root Cause/Conclusion: Still Stands, OFS bulkheads to be used only, new O-Ring

Notes: Liberty_Oxy_RAIL. Got out of the hole and observed that the DC sub had been exposed to fluid on the Tandem sub side, Broke down the tandem sub and bulkhead and noticed a crack in the Tri-V bulkhead.
Description of Incident: Got out of the hole and observed that the DC sub had been exposed to fluid on the Tandem sub side
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Broke down the tandem sub and bulkhead and noticed a crack in the Tri-V bulkhead.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Revised Bulkhead and O-Ring with Tri-V

Notes: Liberty Called at 4:30 this morning. Shot 7 out of 10 guns and obtained a short at gun 8. Had issues reconfiguring due to selecting the wrong number of guns in the string. Walked them through that and were able to continue. They also would like a spare panel out there. They will be purchasing it. I'm headed out to overlook that gun and deliver the panel.
Description of Incident: Supervisor had a short circuit on gun 7 after shooting gun 6. Skipped gun and shot the rest of the stage.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Pressure had bypassed the lower RL Bulkhead, allowing fluid to get into the barrel.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Re-sized O-Ring, increased O.D. on the sealing surface of the Bulkhead.

Notes: Two Failed Switch Stems. One after 4 runs, Mold started to separate. Other Stem, 0 Runs, failed to communicate.
Description of Incident: Two Failed Stems. Crew noticed after 4 runs that the molding was beginning to separate on the top of the Switch Stem. The other failure was noticed when the surface tester did not communicate with the stem.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Molding malfunction on the first stem from Vendor. Pending pickup and investigation on second stem
Root Cause/Conclusion: New vendor has been allocated for molding procedure.

Notes: Caliber CLR called saying they had a short after shooting gun four, were able to get switches back and shot gun five, and then saw an open. They broke the gun down before I got out here and believed there was an issue between our 1 5/8” XO and the bottom of the BRT. After going through the Titan logs I saw they never had a short and were open after the BRT switch. Went through the BRT with their FSM and the banana pin on the attenuating sub was loose.
Description of Incident: Caliber CLR called saying they had a short after shooting gun four, were able to get switches back and shot gun five, and then saw an open. They broke the gun down before I got out here and believed there was an issue between our 1 5/8” XO and the bottom of the BRT. After
Investigation and Troubleshooting: After going through the Titan logs I saw they never had a short and were open after the BRT switch. Went through the BRT with their FSM and the banana pin on the attenuating sub was loose.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Suggested that they tighten the banana pin every time they swap the switch and detonator.

Description of Incident: •	WL Engineer shot 9 guns successfully. On the 10th and final gun WL engineer saw a good break, but when switches were inventoried, the switch was found. Another attempt to shoot the det was made but had an open connection to the detonator.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
•	On the surface, it was confirmed that the gun did not shoot as intended. 
•	Upon inspection of gun #10, it was found that the detonator fired properly but failed to initiate the det cord.
•	With the a140s firing as intended, the most likely causes of this event are as follows: Det fell off of det cord or det was not installed on the det cord. Where damage occurred on the charge tube, the most likely issue was det was to the side of det cord but not installed on the cord.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
Crew retrained
XConnect will move to a newly-engineered det block in the future, which will eliminate det blocks being installed in the field. This will be implemented in Q1.

Notes: Shortly after leaving Caliber/SME, I received a phone call from Caliber/Continental. They were able to set the plug and shoot the first 2 guns then an open circuit was detected after gun 9. Gun 9 was shot to make more holes for pumping down. I arrived on location and the problem was already found. The bulkhead pin was broken off inside of the adjacent switch.
Description of Incident: The engineer was able to set the plug and shoot the first 2 guns then an open circuit was detected after gun 9. Gun 9 was shot to make more holes for pumping down. POOH to investigate.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Once on surface it was found that the the bulkhead pin was broken off inside of the adjacent switch. This was in between gun 8 and 9.
Root Cause/Conclusion: This happened while the crews were prepping there guns on location. They admitted that they were not building as instructed by previous XConnect SQM's. There were two trucks on location. One side was putting them together the right way. Both sides, days/nights were reinstructed on how to build and prep the guns so that this incident does not happen again.

Notes: Mini Bridge Installed Incorrectly
Description of Incident: Caught in the field while doing detonator checks with meter. Det wire not reading anything and through wire was reading the detonator resistance
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Bridge Mini was installed backwards on the end plate
Root Cause/Conclusion: Pictures taken and sent to Tri V and the barrel end plate was replaced and has since been shot.

Notes: Shot guns 1-6 and couldn’t read guns 7-10 showing short
Description of Incident: Shot guns 1-6 then showed short below BRT and not able to read guns 7-10. RBIH and shot 4 clusters to complete the stage.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: The insert in the Direct Connect was cracked. Once the Direct Connect was removed guns 7-10 read.
Root Cause/Conclusion: All the Direct Connect inserts will be taken off location and replaced with ones brought out by hotshot that are 1/8” shorter to prevent them from being able to crack once they are inserted and threaded into the Direct Connect due to being to long.

Notes: This morning Tier 1 had a misrun on stage 20. They set the plug and shot the first gun. The gun then showed “open” after the 2nd gun. On surface the plastic mine bridge mold was broken.
Description of Incident: On stage 20 the engineer set the plug and shot the first gun. When attempting to shoot the 2nd gun the software detected an open. That gun was skipped and the rest of the guns were shot.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: The gun in question was broken down on surface and the mini bridge connection was broken.
Root Cause/Conclusion: There are no preliminary actions at this time.

Notes: Tier 1 over the weekend found metal shavings in the plastic mold again which caused a misrun. They were not able to set the plug. Could only read the BRT and the first 6 guns. This is from an older batch of guns that were sent. This issue is now looked for in house by megging this piece.
Description of Incident: After attempting to set the engineer was only able to read the BRT and the top 6 guns.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: On surface it was found that there was a metal shaving in the mini bridge mold.
Root Cause/Conclusion: The mini bridge connection will be metered in house during the gun building process. This will help catch this issue before the guns are sent to location.

Notes: Gun 7 would not read switch after firing gun 6
Description of Incident: Gun 7 would not read Switch
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Boxed gun on the ground. Gun 7 switch would not read, removed sub and water was present causing the switch to short.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Removed the subs , and gun 7 to be investigated, also took gun 6 to have bulkhead to investigate. Tool will be pressure tested.

Notes: Diagnose barrel that didn't fire, Swap crossovers out, Get Spartan working with IWS, Train supervisors on Spartan
Description of Incident: Detonator went straight short when shooting
Investigation and Troubleshooting: When I arrived the barrel had been torn through by both Nights and Day crews and put back together. I went through every aspect of the barrel with no luck finding anything. Was determined that the detonator failed.
Root Cause/Conclusion: No action needed

Notes: Skipped gun 7
Description of Incident: When attempting to shoot gun seven, he was getting a short. Multiple attempts were made. Skipped gun seven and shot remaining guns.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: When the barrel was broken off a large amount of water poured out. All orings were in place and no visible damage. The water is what caused the short circuit.
Root Cause/Conclusion: The cause of the water entering the barrel was inconclusive and will be sent  for pressure testing

Description of Incident: During the firing process of gun 3 no current or break was observed. Multiple attempts were made with no success. After the last attempt the software indicated No during the deto detection.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Once on surface the test box would not detect the detonator. The detonator read the correct ohms with blaster meter. After looking at the molex connector on the switch, the green wire wasn’t pushed into the connector all the way which created an open circuit to the detonator.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
Talk with the manufacturer about testing these at the factory.

Have gun builders at the shop pull testing these the connections prior to sending out.

Talk to customer about having crews in the field do a light pull test after the detonator is clicked in.

Notes: I received 2 bad single switches back from the field. These were caught in the perf trailer while checking the gun string with the test box. They appear to be two different circuit board types.
Description of Incident: 2 Bad XFire single switches caught in the perf trailer.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
*Switch 4096*
• *Does not read*
• *Over current detected*
This leads me to think there is a short between white and ground. I have not been able to pick it up with a multimeter. I believe this means the short is after communication to the switch starts.

There are couple reasons for voltage regulator to fail:
1- switch was shot many times
2- switch was exposed to high voltage
3- component was bad
4- switch was exposed to esd( again high voltage spike)
*Switch 3976*
• *Reads by itself* 
• *Once a switch is below it, it will not read either.*
• *No switches found or Time out from the test box*
I believe this means a leak between the white and blue wires on the switch. Again, I was not able to pick this up with a multimeter.

Possible reasons;
switch was exposed to high current for too long
Switch was shot too many times
component failure
Root Cause/Conclusion: Replace the guns on location.

Notes: Picked up subs and gun where Bulkhead failed and flooded gun
Description of Incident: Flooded gun.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Bulkhead was observed to have been cut by pressure
Root Cause/Conclusion: Possibly TriV is using a smaller ORing than adequate

Notes: Nate called saying Pintail DBK3 had a misrun. He said that the detonator went but the detonator cord did not initiate.
Description of Incident: Nate called saying Pintail DBK3 had a misrun. He said that the detonator went but the detonator cord did not initiate.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: There was minimal damage to the det cord but the detonator appears to have fully initiated. Based off of the position of the remaining detonator and the minimal damage to the detonator cord; it appears the operator did not ensure the detonator was properly seated in the detonator clip.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Spoke with Pintail FSM and explained the issue and the importance of fully seating the detonator inside the detonator clip.
Incident Summary: On December 21, 2024, a misfire incident was reported by Pintail Completions (Diamondback Energy, Inc.) in the PIN-Midland district. The detonator fired but the cord did not initiate. Investigation concluded the detonator was not properly seated, and the issue has been discussed with the Pintail FSM.

Notes: After setting the plug and shooting gun 1 the engineer received an open circuit message from AWS software. He was unable to regain communication and the decision was made to skip gun 2 and shoot the remaining guns.
Description of Incident: After setting the plug and shooting gun 1 the engineer received an open circuit message from AWS software. He was unable to regain communication and the decision was made to skip gun 2 and shoot the remaining guns.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
While attempting to read the switch at the XConnect facility the open circuit repeated itself in the gun
barrel and once the Rail was removed.
• The Rail was removed, and the switch was verified successfully on its own.
• While inspecting the Scotch-Lock connections and wiring in the rail; the ground wire connection from
the switch to the rail was loose.
o The black ground wire was not fully seated in the crimp causing an intermittent ground that
allowed the switch to check successfully on surface but failed downhole.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Follow up with manufacturer and revisit the QC process.

Notes: Pintail supervisor called stating he had reached plug depth and received a short at gun 4 when trying to read the tool string and arm the plug switch. All trouble shooting steps were tried without success. The decision was made to POOH.
Description of Incident: Pintail supervisor called stating he had reached plug depth and received a short at gun 4 when trying to read the tool string and arm the plug switch. All trouble shooting steps were tried without success. The decision was made to POOH.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
•	Once on surface the tool string still showed an “overcurrent” when checking with the verify box.
•	This short was occurring after the switch and detonator were verified but would not communicate with gun 3.
•	Once gun 4 was removed from the tool string there was evidence of metal shavings in the internal barrel threads as well as some on the tandem sub.
•	The verify box still showed an “overcurrent” on gun 4 when it was tested by itself. 
•	All Scotch-Locks were properly connected, and there were no damaged wires upon initial inspection.
•	After removing the switch cover and wire strap; more metal shavings were discovered on the green, blue, and white wires.
•	After the shavings had been removed the Rail assembly tested successfully with the verify box.
•	The gun and subs were taken back to XConnect for further investigation
Root Cause/Conclusion: At the XConnect facility, none of the “overcurrent” readings were repeated and the switch tested successfully. After breaking down the Rail component-by-component a small cut was observed in the blue wire of the bottom bulkhead. This cut is where the short was occurring and explains why the switch and detonator were being seen on the verify box and unable to communicate with the next gun.

Notes:
Brought back 12 guns from DBK3.

1 was missing a wave spring.

1 needs new sub.

1 was dropped and needs to be rebuilt.

1 had a bent rail.

The remaining six looked and verified good.
The engineer had good switch checks until after tying in. Then he was unable to read past gun 7 (showing a short).
Description of Incident: The engineer had good switch checks until after tying in. Then he was unable to read past gun 7 (showing a short).
Investigation and Troubleshooting: After pulling string back to surface gun 7 was flooded. All O-rings were in place and could not find any damaged O-ring or any debris in the O-ring seat. The barrels were loose, I was able to take one off by hand. The barrel doesn’t appear to be egg shaped or any issue with the sub at first glance.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Will have the subs and barrel pressure tested tomorrow.
Incident Summary: A 'Misrun' incident occurred on December 15, 2024, where an engineer was unable to read past gun 7 due to a short. Initial investigation found gun 7 flooded and loose barrels. Pressure testing of the subs and barrel is scheduled for tomorrow to determine the root cause.

Notes: DBK1 called saying they lost communication after shooting gun 4. Had them restart the computer, panel, etc. with no luck. They tried reading switches through slips with test box and could not communicate. Sounds like an issue in the BRT again but they have been unable to troubleshoot since they have made it back to surface. Heading that way.
Description of Incident: DBK1 called saying they lost communication after shooting gun 4. Had them restart the computer, panel, etc. with no luck. They tried reading switches through slips with test box and could not communicate.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: It looks like they had an intermittent open in the BRT. We were not able to read switches with the verify box through the brt. Removed the brt and we’re seeing an open with the box. They bounced the brt around and got an overcurrent, bounced it again and were able to read the switch.
Root Cause/Conclusion: None at this time
Incident Summary: On December 12, 2024, a Misrun incident was reported by Diamondback Energy, Inc. (customer: Pintail Completions). The incident involved a loss of communication after shooting gun 4, with initial troubleshooting pointing to an intermittent open in the BRT. A root cause has not yet been determined.

Notes: Combo barrel would not read correct ignitor resistance.
Description of Incident:
A Combo switch would not read the correct ignitor resistance. This was found in the trailer.  

I've also updated their software to 21.6 and test box to 5.2.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: We tried multiple firing heads, a stand alone resistor, checked and moved grounding around. Would consistently read open or 3 ohms. I swapped out the switch and it read good.
Root Cause/Conclusion: I swapped out the switch and it read good.
Failed Component: RL XFire Combo Switch - Wired Harness 3/5 ASM

Notes: Horizontal Conoco lost 3 guns down hole on a 2.75” refrac job. I’m at a loss to how or what caused this.
Description of Incident:
•	WL engineer RIH on stage 2 of the A2, had good switch scan multiple times in the well, and sat down at approximately 11,923’ on his first attempt to pump down (below 60 degrees). He was pumping 12 bpm @ max of 4500 #’s.
•	He picked up at 2,000 #’s.
•	WL engineer made a second attempt at the same rate and approximate pressure and sat down 193’ higher at 11,730’ (above 60 degrees).
•	He picked up at 2,650 #’s sticking slightly.
•	WL engineer made a third attempt at the same rate and pressure and sat down 28’ higher than the second attempt at 11,702’.
•	He picked up at 1,800 #’s.
•	WL engineer made a fourth and final attempt at the same rate and pressure and sat down deeper than the second and third attempts at 11,918’.
•	He picked up at 2,100#’s.
•	While POOH the engineer checked switches and received a short after switch four showing a BRT and 5 guns.
•	Once on surface the crew discovered that the third gun’s barrel had separated just below the sub above. Leaving two full guns, the partial third gun, and the pump down sub in the hole.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
•	During the initial frac of Stage 1 pressure dropped from ~8,000 #’s to ~4,000 #’s while pumping.
•	A Dark Vision log has been run on the well and they determined there is damage to the casing. 
•	While the engineer was pumping 12 bpm in 4” he was unable to make it past 60 degrees on any attempt.
•	There have been two previous instances of severe barrel damage (Reference investigation reports dated: 10/21/2024 & 11/08/2024) that show excessive degradation of the metal potentially caused by damaged casing, stuck tools, and fluid/sand jetting.
•	All the detonator blocks in the five returned barrels were melted and there were other indications of excessive heat on other components including melting of the glue in the charge liners.
Root Cause/Conclusion: After examining this event and the two prior events; we believe that while down hole the gun string was subjected to excessive heat and pressure due to the damaged casing. This heat and pressure were enough to weaken the metal of the barrel on gun three to rapidly fail causing the separation of the barrel. In turn this caused an increase in pressure inside the barrel which crushed the internal gun components. Which explains the damage to the top end plate and the partially damaged charge casing that was retrieved in the junk basket run. The top end plate was not damaged in the same manner that is seen with a nominal detonation of a gun and showed similar markings from the previous barrels that had been eroded by pressure. In the previous instances the damage most likely occurred immediately after the guns had been fired or the moment guns were fired, which allowed for the jetting to flow through the perforations and prevented the same failure of the barrel as in this event.

Notes: Got a call from Nate at Pintail. They were unable to set a plug and the resistance went to 25 ohms after the first attempt. They had already started POOH when they called. I got a hold of the engineer and explained what I thought it was and the company man let him pump back down and they set the plug successfully. It was a Probe shooting panel this time, Rail, and Mach 33 Ignitor.
Description of Incident: Got a call from Nate at Pintail. They were unable to set a plug and the resistance went to 25 ohms after the first attempt.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: They had already started POOH when they called. I got a hold of the engineer and explained what I thought it was and the company man let him pump back down and they set the plug successfully. It was a Probe shooting panel this time.
Root Cause/Conclusion: It appears that the resistor burned but current was not held long enough to fully break the resistor. We will be making a preliminary bulletin to distribute to the crews on the proper shooting profile for firing the Mach 33.
Incident Summary: An incident occurred on December 11, 2024, involving Pintail Completions and Diamondback Energy, Inc. The issue was an inability to set a plug, with resistance going to 25 ohms. The problem was resolved, and the plug was successfully set after pumping back down. The root cause appears to be a resistor burning without sufficient current. A preliminary bulletin will be issued to crews on the proper shooting profile for the Mach 33.

Notes: Misrun due to exposed brass on the mini bridge.
Description of Incident: Set the plug and shot 1 gun and had a short detected on gun 9. The engineer was able to shoot 2 more guns while detecting an intermittent short. Unfortunately no more guns were able to be shot. They proceeded to pull out of the well and run in with a new gun string.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: During the investigation it was found that there was exposed brass on the mini bridge. This in turn caused a short in the tool string.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Will check with vendor to ensure the quality of the product . Also the connection will be metered in house during the gun building process.

Notes: Had one cluster in two different stages that didn’t shoot and showed a short when attempting to shoot.
Description of Incident: Failed mini-bridge
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Brass protruding through on the detonator side of the mini- bridge which created a short circuit when attempting to fire.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Start using High voltage meter checks on Mini-bridges before they are installed into the end plates.

Notes: Short detected in the gun string.
Description of Incident: Could not set the plug due to a short being detected in the gun string.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: There was a piece of exposed brass on the mini bridge connection.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Will check with vendor to ensure the quality of the product . Also the connection will be metered in house during the gun building process.

Notes: Short after attempting gun 1. Couldn’t read past gun 3
Description of Incident: Set plug and seen a break shooting gun 1 then couldn’t read gun 2. System showed a short after gun 3. Skipped gun 2 and once he was OOH gun 1 didn't fire either.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: More than likely there was a leak somewhere in gun 3 switch on the blue wire side. Guns 1 and 2 still read good once the tandem sub between guns 2 and 3 was removed. The tandem sub was shorted of course due to gun 3 being shot. I think he had a small leak somewhere and after setting the plug and when he attempted to shoot gun 1 the voltage applied to the leak got worse and wasn’t able to read past gun 3 switch. More than likely in the switch but can’t say definitely since the sub was damaged.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Nothing else could have been done to prevent the issue. Everything was checked and good until voltage and current was applied.

Notes: 2 guns on tool string failed to transfer
Description of Incident: When they got OOH, 2 of the 10 clusters didn't fire
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Det cord length was sufficient, det did not low order, and failed to transfer to the det cord. The end of the cord was deformed from the explosion and the end of the charge tube was damaged from the detonator.  Apparently, the detonator was installed backwards where the lead azide charge in the detonator and direction of the blast was directed away from the explosive train and at the end of the det cord where there was less RDX powder.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
Broke down the guns they had built on location and re-installed the detonators the correct way while training the crews. Had some detonator end caps sent out to put on the end of the det cord to prevent the RDX powder from falling out and prevent the det block from coming off the det cord in the event the det cord is too short.

Engineering has made a det bracket that will eliminate the need of the current det blocks on the DSX gun system having to be installed on the det cord that will prevent human error.  We are waiting on these to be manufactured.
Incident Summary: On December 5, 2024, a misfire incident occurred where 2 of 10 clusters failed to fire. Investigation found that detonators were installed incorrectly. Crews were retrained, detonators re-installed, and preventative end caps were provided. Engineering is also developing a new bracket to eliminate this human error.

Notes: Gun did't go off
Description of Incident: Once they came off the well after shooting stage 4, they seen one barrel didn't fire. The det did not transfer to the det cord.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: The detonator did not low order. The detonator cord was a sufficient length. The end of the cord was mushroomed, and the end of the charge tube was flared some from the blast. The detonator was installed backwards on the det cord where the explosive end of the det which contains the lead azide was at the end of the det cord with less powder and the directional explosion of the A-140 was directed away from the explosive train.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
Day crew had built that gun that night crew shot. I asked the night crew if they were doing them that way or the way I showed them where the top of the det was facing up hole. The said they did them the correct way and day crew had 3-gun strings left built. I advised them to shoot the gun strings they had built if they installed the detonators correctly, set day shift guns to the side and I would wait on day shift to arrive and break the ones they did down with them and retrain them.
On an engineering front we have a det bracket that won't require the guys in the field to install det blocks directly on the det cord to eliminate this causing any future problems.
Incident Summary: On December 4, 2024, a misfire occurred for Pintail Completions at PIN-Midland, operating for Diamondback Energy, Inc., where a gun barrel failed to fire. Investigation revealed the detonator was installed backwards due to an assembly error by the day crew. Retraining is planned, and an engineering solution (det bracket) is being considered to prevent future incidents.

Notes: Gun 1 went off instead of plug
Description of Incident: Attempted to set plug with no indication and then couldn’t read past gun 2 and showed a short. Came OOH and gun 1 had shot.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Looked at the log file. The plug IP address was activated when gun 1 fired.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Sent log files and shot plot to AWS engineering to look at and find the possible cause.
Failed Component: RL XFire Combo Switch - Wired Harness 3/5 ASM

Notes: Received a call at 11:00 pm from Pintail DBK1. Engineer stated he was pulling heavy after completing the stage and was worried the plug hadn’t set. He explained that he fired the igniter and the shot plot looked good, lost 180 #’s, and that the well pressured up when they started pumping on it. He said he was concerned because after he tried setting the plug he had a reading of 12 Ohms on the igniter but they decided to continue with the stage because of the weight loss and pressure increase. They did pull the plug back to surface. Waiting on FSM to arrive and we will investigate further.
Description of Incident: Received a call at 11:00 pm from Pintail DBK1. Engineer stated he was pulling heavy after completing the stage and was worried the plug hadn’t set. He explained that he fired the igniter and the shot plot looked good, lost 180 #’s, and that the well pressured up when they started pumping on it. He said he was concerned because after he tried setting the plug he had a reading of 12 Ohms on the igniter but they decided to continue with the stage because of the weight loss and pressure increase. They did pull the plug back to surface. Waiting on FSM to arrive and we will investigate further.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Igniter did not fire.
Looking through the log the software showed a resistance of 51 Ohms at the start of the stage. The igniter reads ~13 Ohms with the meter, in the firing head, and by itself after the run on surface.
This seems similar to the run yesterday on DBK1 where the engineer made the first attempt and had a resistance reading of 11 Ohms and again after the second failed attempt. He was able to set the plug on the third attempt.
In this incident the engineer only made one attempt, assumed the plug set from weight loss (that most likely is due to being toe up. I witnessed a 100#+ weight loss while he was getting ready to set the second plug on the same stage), and continued shooting guns.
This was stage 49 on the 16 WA on the Abu Garcia pad.
Root Cause/Conclusion: The igniter is being sent back to Diamondback for further investigation. Igniter successfully fired, at manufactruing facility. Determined to be user error on not allowing enough time on firing mode.
Incident Summary: On November 25, 2024, a misfire incident was reported by Pintail Completions for Diamondback Energy, Inc. An engineer was concerned a plug had not set, leading to the plug being pulled back to surface. The investigation concluded that the igniter successfully fired, and the root cause was determined to be user error due to not allowing enough time in firing mode.

Notes: SFR Kraken Lost full inventory of their switches at the Curve, Second Run lost them at the plug switch, third run lost them at gun 5. Replaced entire toolstring and Top Sub and made a successful run.
Description of Incident: SFR Kraken Lost full inventory of their switches at the Curve, Second Run lost them at the plug switch, third run lost them at gun 5. Replaced entire toolstring and Top Sub and made a successful run.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
3.	Description of Incident:
•	During the first run, Surefire was unable to inventory the full gun string at the Curve. Multiple attempts were made to re-establish communication, resulting in varying numbers of switches being inventoried, but the full string was never achieved.
•	The second run lost inventory at the plug switch.
•	The third run lost inventory at Gun 5.
•	The entire gun string and top sub were replaced, leading to a successful run.


4.	Investigation and Troubleshooting:
•	  Initial assessment indicated inventory issues during multiple attempts to kickoff, with varying results. Suspected main line connection issues, including Line and Head BRT.
•	  On arrival, a tandem sub was found leaking at the bottom of Gun 2. No spares were available on location.
o	  While the new sub was being obtained, the gun string was verified using a Surface Tester. The team decided to omit the faulty gun and not run a spare.
•	 The second run failed to inventory the combo switch at the curve. Despite explanations, the supervisor suspected the switch as the root cause.
o	  A new combo switch was installed in the bottom gun, Line was reheaded, BRT was rebuilt and the Swivel was replaced. A Third attempt was made.
•	The Third run failed to inventory the full gun string as well. 5 switches consistently were the result. 
o	XC was dispatched back to the yard to provide a new gun string, panels and cables.
•	New gun string and new Direct Connect were swapped in and the next run was successful. 
•	The short was found on the upper face of the bulkheads, where the retainer nut meets the bulkhead.
•	Composite material on the bulkheads was worn away due to incorrect installation processes.
•	 Investigation revealed:
o	Incorrect Impact Tool: The assembly team used thread protector removal impact tool instead of the assembly impact tool.
Inconsistent Installation Practices: Some employees tightened bulkheads excessively, while others stopped prematurely.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
•  Training Conducted:
•	Employees were trained on the proper use of assembly impact tools.
•	Installation processes were standardized, emphasizing the risks of inconsistent torque application.
•  Faulty Components:
•	All faulty bulkheads (Tri-V models) were isolated for further analysis.

Notes: The engineer set the plug, shot gun 1, and became stuck. The decision was made to continue shooting, they shot three more guns, and became free. The engineer POOH and on surface saw there was damage to gun 1-4.
Description of Incident: The engineer set the plug, shot gun 1, and became stuck. The decision was made to continue shooting, they shot three more guns, and became free. The engineer POOH and on surface saw there was damage to gun 1-4.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Once on surface, it was found that on gun #1, #2, #3, and #4 there was severe corrosion or wear,
leading to structural damage and holes that appear to have been created on the outside of the barrel
working into the barrel. The metal wear, damage, and holes were all within approximately 1.5” of the
nearest perforation. It seems the most severe damage was closest to the second perforation of each
barrel and gun #4 seems to have received the most damage of all.

XConnect was made aware of the issue to assist in troubleshooting. Looking through the barrel, it is
the consensus on the XConnect side that the damage to the barrel was caused by some sort of
external force, i.e., extremely rapid release of pressure or heat from the well bore. This was
concluded since all the damage was to the outside of the barrel. All charge holes on the barrel were
observed and did not look to be the cause of damage.

Judging by the shape charge exit hole on the barrel, the tool string
moved roughly 1.5 inches before being hit by this event of rapid heat/pressure release.

The charge vendor was contacted and came to the same conclusion, stating they had never seen
damage like this.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Watch for similar incidents and send the damaged barrels to a metallurgist.

Notes: Last night, Devon had an igniter that did not fire. They shot the remaining guns because they were unsure if the plug was set. Once the tool string was returned to the surface, it was found that the igniter still metered properly. Ethan also found that he was able to shoot through switch.
Description of Incident: Last night, Devon had an igniter that did not fire. They shot the remaining guns because they were unsure if the plug was set. Once the tool string was returned to the surface, it was found that the igniter still metered properly. Ethan also found that he was able to shoot through switch.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Confirmed that you are in fact able to shoot through this switch as long as you aren’t shorted to igniter wire.
What I found is that with gun 1 armed there is voltage to the igniter. So what is happening as you shoot gun 1 you are actually shorted to the igniter.
What I don’t know yet is what causes this issue. There is nothing on the board or wires that I would say is a short. It must be something with the gates opening incorrectly when gun 1 is armed.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Change Dual Stem checking procedure

Notes: XC was notifed that the crew had observed two guns where the white wire was pulled out of the Scotchlock. Cynthia Solis had went to location to go through the guns and had found two more and repaired them. As we have seen before. White wire especially soft wire, if not left with adequate slack will produce this issue.
Description of Incident: Customer could not get verification on surface with two separate guns. They pulled det covers off noticed the white wire was removed from the ScotchLok.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: This stems from an error in wiring when not enough slack is given to the wire,especially with the combination of the softer and or smaller wire provided.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Wire to be given proper length and routed outside of the wire cover.

Notes: While already on location for training of XC. The engineer completed a run but once on surface he found that the plug was still attached and had not been set. We helped troubleshoot with him and determined he had not rolled enough current to the igniter for long enough.
Description of Incident: The engineer returned tool string to surface and realized the plug had not in fact been set downhole. We were already on location for training on XC gun system, and helped him troubleshoot the firing head and ignitor.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Once the tool string was back to surface we found the ignitor had not burned, and that the firing head and setting tool had not flooded. We verified the switch and ignitor with the VeriFire box, metered the ignitor in the firing head, verified the switch alone with the VeriFire box, metered the switch independently, and metered the ignitor independently; all without issue.  We proceeded to check the VI plot on Control Fire and noticed that the engineer did not roll into the ignitor with enough current for a long enough time frame to burn the resistor. He also did not roll the variac all the way down and tried to apply voltage again for a second attempt. This gave the engineer a false belief that his ignitor was showing open. He further confused the situation by trying to pump down and sit on the plug and assumed a loss of 200 pounds of tension was verification the plug had been set.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
The company rep for Formentera had him pump a second tool string to plug depth and set a plug. This attempt was successful and the engineer had applied more voltage resulting in a higher amperage breaking the resistor and setting the plug successfully. We spoke with Tim Carlyle (FSM) about what  we found and he agreed; and had a conversation with the engineer.

We were able to address the same stem at the shop and fire the ignitor successfully.

Notes: Received a call that Pintail had a short in their gun string after equalization and requested for us to come out. Found there were missing O-rings on both sides of gun sub.
Description of Incident: Pintail crew verified gun string with verify box on surface, checked the gun string through Xfire, and again after bumping up in lubricator. All checks were successful. Once pressure test of 8,000 PSI and equalization was complete; the gun string failed a verification through Xfire. Augie (FSM) called stating they had a short above the bottom gun and requested our presence on location.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Once we arrived on location we broke the gun string above the last gun and tandem sub. The sub looked good without any issue. We then broke the tandem sub off of the bottom gun (topside) and found the gun to have flooded. We then broke the bottom gun from the firing head and found the 230 O-rings were missing from the tandem sub (bottom side) to the firing head. After removing the tandem sub from the gun we found the internal 230 O-rings were also missing. After removing the firing head from the shorty setting tool we found the setting tool had also flooded. The O-ring seats on both sides of the flooded tandem sub showed clear evidence that the original O-rings had been installed previously. After speaking with the night crew (who assembled the gun string) they informed us that the gun did not verify after assembly and in order to investigate they had removed the O-rings from the tandem sub to help re-install the sub more easily. Once they were able to verify the gun string they had forgotten they removed the O-rings and continued with the gun string assembly.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Pintail had a conversation with their crews and implemented an FSM on location for both day and night shifts.

Notes: Bad XFerno Ignitor caused a misrun.
Description of Incident: The engineer saw no sign of a successful plug set. He had no tension drop or current break.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
The ignitor meters. .04 ohm to 50.0 ohms. It meter readings change after tapping the ignitor on the table.

Likely scenario:
Brass parts are not getting adequate nickel plating inside the 0.81mm bore
Tinned wire against the exposed brass with a combination of ammonia/moisture causing galvanic reaction and corrosion
Root Cause/Conclusion: A temporary fix has been done by Diamondback by using a double crimp and ensuring proper nickel coating. A revision has been made to the bulkhead assembly to allow for a solder area.

Notes: Attempted to fire an Inferno igniter. The supervisor fired and questioned the plot (figure 1). BHA was returned to surface, and it was verified that the igniter failed to initiate. The crew changed BHA and reran into the well and completed the stage
Description of Incident: Attempted to fire an Inferno igniter. The supervisor fired and questioned the plot (figure 1). BHA was returned to surface, and it was verified that the igniter failed to initiate. The crew changed BHA and reran into the well and completed the stage
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
After reviewing the shot plot it was determined that the igniter didn’t encounter correct current.  The shot plot displays a flat current draw. Most firing plots display an erratic current plot as the voltage is applied to the resisters in the igniter circuit. 
Test Performed:
1.	Metered igniter.
a.	Igniter measured 52.3Ω
2.	Tested XFire Combo stem
a.	Both switches’ circuits were found correct 
3.	Tested XFire Combo and Igniter circuit
a.	Both switches’ circuits were found correct 
b.	Igniter resistance was read correctly
After testing was finished, the igniter was removed from the firing head for examination. The firing head was found to have excess carbon, grease and rust buildup in the housing
Root Cause/Conclusion: Crew needs to take extra time to clean the firing heads

Notes: Helped loading guns. Found 4 ground brushes missing and wire out of molex terminal.
Description of Incident: While training RMWS gun loaders. 4 assemblies were found without ground brushes and 2 click in det terminals were broken.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: During the build process, charge tubes were being pulled from a cart to install the ground brushes and then placed back onto the same cart. This would mix the charge tubes that had ground brushes and did not have them on the same cart.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
XConnect has made a change to the ground brush installation procedure. Once a ground brush is installed, it is placed onto a different cart. This will ensure finished and non-finished charge tubes do not get mixed together.

Molex connectors are to be pull tested during assembly. The manufacture is going to automate the process of pushing the wires into the connectors.

Notes: New Unit had XFire Panels Installed by TWG Personnel. Software was not deriving tension from SDS Winch Panel. XFire panel was swapped with no success. It was later determined that the signal ouput was below reference values. New parameters were established and tension was recalibrated.
Description of Incident: No tension derived into Spartan Software. Panel was installed by TWG technician
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Replaced P2000 with P2500. Results were the same. While on surface I was not seeing any signal into tension input and communication voltage on transducer cable appeared to be background readings. While downhole with more weight on load cell it was checked again, realizing signal was being transmitted but it was very low. Opened up signal parameters and recalibrated tension.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Ensure customers winch panel type. Make sure all Red Lion and SDS WInch panels are setup with 15 VDC Tension inputs.

Notes: New tandem subs are galling.
Description of Incident: New XC tandem subs are galling
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
We believe dust and dirt are getting onto the seal surface of the barrel at the XC facility. When you screw a new tandem sub on with tight tolerance the dust and dirt is pushed forward by the leading oring causing a buildup on the first landing of the tandem sub. Then guns are shot and the dirt gets smashed into the landing. When you unscrew the sub the dirt causes galling to start happening.

Will update if a different conclusion is found.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Ask the field personnel to wipe the seal surface of the barrel before installing the subs. Clean the seal surface of the barrels at the shop before capping them.

Switches were removed from the guns for further investigation. While metering diode, continuity and insulation,  it was determined that gun 7 switch was leaking to ground.

Notes: Called to location to help diagnose a problem with setting the plug. NexTier management could not find an issue and asked for assistance.
Description of Incident: NexTier suffered a Misrun due to the inability to successfully set the plug on Stage 13 of the Thorpe 8-6H well. Three firing attempts were made before making the decision to POOH. XConnect was then called to location to help assist in diagnosing.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Upon arriving to location I was presented with a Firing head with a Mach33 Igniter installed and connected to a
Tandem Sub. 

•Igniter resistance was measuring at 16.7Ω.
•Tandem Sub was removed and Igniter was metered in the housing measuring 50.4Ω
•Tandem Sub was visually inspected, metered for continuity and an insulation test was performed. All resulting successful.
•Bulkhead was removed from the tandem sub and visually inspected, metered and insulation tested successfully.
•Tandem sub was then put back together and reconnected to the firing head. Resulting in successful tests. Never to fail again. 
•The gun and switches were then inspected. Surface tester inventoried switches, inspection of wiring and connections, bottom rail terminal and ground wire all resulted successful assembly and operation. 
•The two switches were then removed by NexTier Rep to be sent in for further diagnosis.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Materials will be sent in to manufacture for further diagnosis.

Notes: Two Switches were isolated for a short circuit in gun 5. A misrun was resulted in the short
Description of Incident: While attempting to fire gun 5 the system was showing a short circuit. Gun 5 was skipped and the rest of the string was shot successfully.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Switch was brought to XC Facility for further diagnosis. 

•During the initial meter check it was found that the Green detonator leg was shorted out to ground. This was also verified on the XFire Stem Testing Unit

•The Stem was then melted down to determine wire placement, terminal placement and wire damage. All factors were passing. 

• After the Stem was melted down unfortunately it lost all functionality. No Diode reading, Det leg was free of ground short, and the loss of software function. 

Although it is not clear, it is possible that the faults came within the board itself, and after being melted down components were altered.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Newly arrived XFire Testing Unit will help the effectiveness of the QC Process of Single stems in accuracy and eliminating human error factors.

Notes: Switch presented No Detonator while inventoried on surface. Switch was swapped out and Gun String was ran sucessfully.
Description of Incident: Crew attempted to check a newly built gun string using XFire Surface Tester. Single switch detected no detonator. The switch was replaced with another resulting in a successful test.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Switch was brought to XC Facility for further diagnosis. A series of meter check were performed resulting successful. The Switch was then hooked up to XFire panel for further testing.

• Results showed that the Deto Detect function did not work, but did in fact allow for the firing sequence to be initiated.
 
•Firing voltage was applied to the detonator and voltage measurement was successful.

         Root Cause for the issue is internally in the switch board's parameters for detecting a change in resistance on the detonator leg allowing the software to yield accurate results.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Switch will be sent to manufacturer for further diagnosis.

Notes: Call was made to XC. Instructed to pickup a XFire Double Stem that was involved in a Misrun. Gun 1 was fired instead of the Plug.
Description of Incident: GO Engineer deployed gunstring to Plug set depth. Plug switch was armed and firing sequence was initiated. No indications of the plug being set were noticed. Engineer lost communication of Gun 1 and Plug Switch.  Engineer then deployed the gunstring to surface and noticed that Gun #1 had initially fired instead of the Ignitor
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Double Stem was brought to the XC Facility. Testing began with connecting the stem to the XFire panel and obtaining the inventory of the two switches in the stem. 

The plug switch was then armed and a firing sequence was initiated with a meter being connected to the Ignitor leg to determine voltage applied. 225v was applied to the switch and the output to the Ignitor was 0v. The meter was then connected to the Detonator leg of the Gun switch where the voltage output was measured at 225v. The reverse was then performed, arming the gun switch and metering the output to the Detonator leg and then the Ignitor. Resulting in the opposite issue. It is determined that in manufacturing of the Double Stem the two switches were placed into the bracket and the two green detonator wires were routed incorrectly. 

Plug Switch green wire was routed to the Detonator Output and the Gun Switch green wire was routed to the Ignitor. The other failure point was in the QC Process at the XC Facility. Inspectors run through a QC Process to determine if this or any other type of failure or any other type of failure is presented in a Double Stem. This one made it through inspection somehow.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
•A discussion was had with the QC and Operations team at the XC facility on the QC Process and the fundamentals of how and why the process is in place. 

•Procurement will be having a discussion with the manufacturer on the failure.

•An adequate inventory of XFire Combo Switches is being obtained to allow for Single Switch Plug Stem manufacturing.

Notes: Flooded BRT. (lost switches downhole)
Description of Incident: XC was called by the engineer as he had lost all switches at 17k feet. Trouble shooting was given over the phone by Derik and Kelly.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Multiple surface test were ran from using the logger next to it to try and read switches and using the surface test box to try and read through the slips. It was noted that a high milliamp was seen while trying to read the switches. We had intermittent switch readings once we got in the vertical. On surface we could read the switches with the test box. Although extremely high milliamps were seen.  Found water in the BRT. All oring's were present and intact.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Remove this section of the BRT from service for tolerance checks.

Notes: Shot gun 1 and couldn’t shoot gun 2. Switch read after attempting to shoot and had to skip gun 2. Brought 2 more barrels out and had to RBIH and shoot 1 cluster.
Description of Incident: Set plug and shot gun 1. When attempting to shoot gun 2, the engineer said the current came up with voltage and didn’t break showing a short. After the first attempt she switch in gun 2 still reads he tried a second time and still read the switch after shooting and then proceeded to skip gun 2 and shoot the remaining clusters on the gun string. Description of Incident: No deto detected with surface test box. Purple wire of the mini bridge meters open.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: An open is found when metering through the purple wire of the mini bridge. This was found to be a problem during manufacturing with the bullet terminal of a different DSC.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Xconnect has changed the style of the internal wire connection being done. This will eliminate the problem with new inventory.

Notes: Bad Single Stem. No Deto Detected.
Description of Incident: A new XFire single stem shows no deto detected. This was caught in the trailer on the test box.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Switch stem 633B was brought to the XConnect facility for investigation. The stem meters correctly. I then
hooked it up and shot through the stem and saw voltage at the detonator terminal.

After that I hooked up a 50-ohm resistor to the detonator lead and still saw No Deto detected. I saw the
same thing with an older version of the firmware. I also had No Deto detected with a surface test box.

Lastly, I shot through the switch and the resistor burned. This tells me the switch is working, but something is wrong with the deto detection on the switch.

After talking with our supplier, there is a "resistance threshold" that the detonator detection is based on. It
is possible one of the resistors on the switch board is reading higher than it should and this is causing the
improper reading.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
Our supplier is going to get with his engineering team to try and understand how this happened. Continue
to monitor with the surface test box.

Notes: GO Wireline encountered a Misrun at Plug Shoot Depth due to not being able to inventory all switches in the string. XC was notified of the failure and obtained the Stem in question. XC was notified that the Stem had a total of 8 successful runs prior to its failure. There were no marks or indicators on the stem to verify.
Description of Incident: GO Wireline encountered a Misrun at Plug Shoot Depth due to not being able to inventory all switches in the string. XC was notified of the failure and obtained the Stem in question. XC was notified that the Stem had a total of 8 successful runs prior to its failure. There were no marks or indicators on the stem to verify.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Upon receiving the Stem a series of QC checks were performed.  • Verified the Ground Plungers were in working order. • Verified Ground continuity. (Fig1) • Observed Stem molding is free from cracks or air pockets • Verified Terminals were free of debris, and appear to be of correct size, indicating correct uninstall procedure. • Verified Thru terminal had no leakage to ground. • Verified Deto terminal had no leakage to ground. • Verified diode voltage drop, 0.48V. (Fig2) The switch was then connected the switch stem to an XFire panel and attempted to inventory. The switch would not verify in the system, Indicating an internal error in the switch board itself. It was then melted down to determine if it could be due to stem molding and terminal connection. All indications show that the terminal connections and wiring were adequate.  Switch will be sent to manufacturer for further diagnosis.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Switch will be sent to manufacturer for further investigation

Notes: A single stem displayed a failure to register the detonator
Description of Incident: A single stem displayed a failure to register the detonator
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
The stem underwent testing procedures at the Midland, TX office, including scanning and examination. Upon scanning, it was determined that the detonator circuit was defective. Subsequently, a simulated detonator was integrated and scanned, yielding identical outcomes—indicating the absence of a functional detonator. 
Notably, the stem was fired without the resistor burning.
The stem was melted and investigated internally.  No assembly or wiring issues were encountered
Root Cause/Conclusion: None at this time

Notes: Top Rail Gun had Pinched Thru Wire. Resulting in a Misrun
Description of Incident: NexTier suffered a misrun after deploying to 200’ and initiating an inventory check on the gun string. Multiple attempts were made with intermittent success to obtain a full and consistent inventory. The call was made to pull the gun string out of the well.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: It was observed that the top gun in the string had a Thru-Wire pinched between the det cover and the ballast (Fig1). The switch has wires coming from both ends, the white was folded back and over itself creating a tighter fit when installing the cover. Green line indicates the correct pathway to be followed
Root Cause/Conclusion:
•	Discussion with the buid team and personnel in charge of the QC process, ensuring all steps are followed and if revisions need to be made to the process.
•	XConnect has a new Rail Cover in production and new Wire Strap in production

Notes: 1-5/8"x1-7/8" Universal Adapter Cutting QC O-Rings
Description of Incident: Crew had noticed O-Rings were being cut on their Quick Change while they were installing into the Universal Adapter.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Adapters in question were brought back to the facility as well as the current inventory stock and were isolated for QC checks. The operations team took multiple Direct Connect Quick Changes and Universal Adapters and threaded them together in attempt to recreate the issue. All three adapters failed the check due to creating damage to the O-Rings on the Quick Change. It was noticed that the hole machined for the Set-Screw had a sharp edge instead of a bevel/chamfer at the transition. It was noticed that the O-ring would catch at this point of the sharp edge of the hole and would create the damage in question.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Operations team manually cleaned up the sealing surface and the transition into the Set-Screw hole allowing for a smooth surface for the O-Ring to install into.

Description of Incident: Day crew gun left for night shift. The top gun would not shoot. First attempt he got current but switch was still there. Also, the new pin wire connector dets that will be coming out would eliminate this misrun. Although they should still check resistance with new style detonators more than likely it wouldn’t have caused a misrun.

Notes:
Upon arrival at the designated location at 0500 hours, the night shift supervisor was instructing the day supervisor regarding the operation of the software and the functionalities of the XFire system. The day supervisor possessed a basic understanding of the system's operation. 
 
 Run One 
 
 The first gun run was initiated, and a switch scan was performed at this time. Additional scans were made at tie-in and prior to pumpdown. The supervisor pulled into plug depth and scanned the switches to set the plug. A positive plug set was indicated. The supervisor commenced shooting the first zone, but he was unsure of the scan/firing procedure; I instructed the supervisor during the shooting phase. The first gun was shot stationary due to the short spacing from the plug to the first shot. Then, the 10-gun string ASF/POOH was shot. 
 
 During the plug set operation, my focus was on the software issue that we encountered the previous day*(Tier 1 OK). I was checking for the two windows to confirm the plug set. 
 
 Note*: During the plug switch scan, the Company Man expressed dissatisfaction with the performance of the XFire system from his experience the previous day. 
 
 Run Two 
 
 Gun run two was RIH, and a switch scan was performed by the supervisor. Additional scans were made at tie-in and prior to pumpdown. The supervisor pulled into plug depth and scanned the switches to set the plug. A positive plug set was indicated. The first gun was shot stationary due to the short spacing from the plug to the first shot. During the firing sequence of gun two, the supervisor scanned the switches again. He fired gun two before completing the scan sequence, from the scan it appeared to fire. He Continued to shoot the remaining gun string; he called all eleven shots as fired off his shot sheet. 
 
 I reviewed the switch scans to confirm gun two fired; I visually inventoried the fired switches and found gun eleven was not fired. The gun was confirmed un- fired on the surface. The supervisor and I met with the Company Man to review the situation with the unfired gun. We did not delve into details on the cause of the failure at this time. The Company Man replied that "he can change out equipment." 
 
 Note: During run two, I noticed that the supervisor was rescanning switches after each shot. I assumed there was an issue with the auto-scan. I called Eric and discussed the issue, and he said it was a procedural error by the supervisor. This was an extra unneeded scan. I instructed the supervisor to eliminate the extra scan. 
 
  
 
  
 
  
 
  
 
 Run Three 
 
 Prior to the start of run three, I was informed that the Company Man requested Hunting switches be used on the remaining job. A hotshot was initiated, and switches were on location at ~1300 hours. Gun run two was RIH, and a switch scan was performed by the supervisor. Additional scans were made at tie-in and prior to pumpdown. The supervisor pulled into plug depth and scanned the switches to set the plug. A positive plug set was indicated. ASF/POOH. 
 
 A Tier 1 FSM arrived to help transition to the Titan system. I removed the XFire system and helped install the Hunting panel. I reviewed with the crew the potential issue of having two switch systems on location and the possibility of the wrong switch being installed. All XFire switches were isolated from the loading area. During testing of the switches with the Hunting panel, switches could not be read. I asked the FSM if he changed the BRT XFire switch to a Hunting switch. A Hunting switch was installed in the BRT. All switch communications evaluated good.

Notes: WL engineer went to shoot the igniter, but gun one was shot instead.
Description of Incident: WL engineer went to shoot the igniter, but gun one was shot instead.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
The switch was confirmed wired backward when doing a surface test with the hunting 
 
 control fire software. The manufacturer wired the switch with the incorrect 
 
 green in the downhole position. The green wire from 
 
 the plug switch was in the up position towards the det, 
 
 and the green wire from gun 1 was in the down position 
 
 towards the igniter. Although rare, this error is checked 
 
 for in the double stem QC process at the XConnect 
 
 facility
Root Cause/Conclusion:
The manufacturer will be informed of the issue, and the XConnect quality 
 
 control team will be retrained on the SOP. 
 
 In the future, all XFire doubles will be made with a combo switch (one switch 
 
 that shoots det and igniter), eliminating this issue

Notes: After shooting two guns, the WL engineer was unable to read past this switch.
Description of Incident: After shooting two guns, the WL engineer was unable to read past this switch.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: When the switch was returned to XConnect, it was found that the blue (down communication bullet terminal) was pushed into mold ~1/8th of an inch. We have replicated this issue in the shop by slightly bending the top bulkhead on the gun below. The flat face on the top bulkhead banana pin catches the ledge of the female socket in the stem and pushes it inward. On surface, you still have a bit of face contact so that it will pass surface checks, but once you start shooting guns, it loses continuity.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Although this issue is rare, we are looking at increasing the lead taper on the ID of our bullet terminal. We have informed our assembly crew of the importance of checking for bent pins. If field crews find bent pins or unintentionally bend top bulkhead pins, it is crucial that they do not run that gun.

Notes: On July 4th XTO Fleet suffered a Misrun when attempting to shoot Gun 1 & Gun 9 subsequently initiated.
Description of Incident: On July 4th XTO Fleet suffered a Misrun when attempting to shoot Gun 1 & Gun 9 subsequently initiated.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Ranger SQM obtained the Single Switch Stem for internal testing. Basic meter checks were performed in addition to checking for current leakage between ground and the series of terminals. All results were satisfactory. Once XConnect received the Stem (OAh Version 1.8) the same tests were performed with no difference in results. The Stem was then melted down to expose the Switch and wiring. At that point it was visible that there was an exposure in the insulation on the Green Wire. The placement of the exposure is right at the bend where it is closest to the Blue Wire terminal during the molding process. This exposure most likely allowed for current to be exposed to the Green (Deto) wire while initiating Gun 1.
Root Cause/Conclusion: Revisions in the Molding Process have already been put into effect. This version of the Single Switch Stem is that of an older batch. This risk factor is believed to already been mitigated.

Notes:
Engineer received an open circuit warning after gun for at KOP. The decision was made to pull out of the hole 
 
 to swap gun strings.
Description of Incident:
Engineer received an open circuit warning after gun for at KOP. The decision was made to pull out of the hole 
 
 to swap gun strings.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
On surface, it was found that the top bulkhead was pulled out of the top-end plate causing the wire 
 
 on top-bulk to break. 
 
 This can happen on LynX guns if the charge tube is seated in the sub above and then pulled back out 
 
 of the sub. With the top-bulkhead dislodged from the top-end plate, the charge tube was re-seated 
 
 in the sub above.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
Best ways to avoid this in future: 
 
 • Grease top-bulkhead O-rings. Having the top-bulkhead properly lubricated will assist 
 
 in the top bulkhead not getting stuck. 
 
 • If the charge tube needs to be pulled back out always pull it all the way out of barrel 
 
 so the top bulkhead can be inspected. If it pulls out of top endplate it can be sent back to XConnect to be fixed.
Failed Component: XC Wired Big Bulkhead w/ 36" White Lead ASM

Notes:
While shooting gun 7, gun 13 was shot, causing a loss of communication from gun 8 to gun 13. A double break 
 
 was observed, indicating that the intended gun was fired then switch (D5H) shorted to green wire causing gun 
 
 number 13 to fire
Description of Incident:
While shooting gun 7, gun 13 was shot, causing a loss of communication from gun 8 to gun 13. A double break 
 
 was observed, indicating that the intended gun was fired then switch (D5H) shorted to green wire causing gun 
 
 number 13 to fire
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Initial findings: 
 
 • 4 run marks on single switch stem, indicating this was the 5th run on the switch. 
 
 • No issues were found when checking the switch (D5H) with the blaster's meter. Indicating that the 
 
 short was not substantial enough to short out at low voltage. Short only occurred at higher voltage 
 
 due to arcing. 
 
 • The switch (D5H) was connected to hunting titan software at XConnect’s shop to mimic the issue seen 
 
 in the field. With a switch below armed, it was observed that the switch did, in fact, allow voltage to 
 
 detonator when the switch was not armed.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
The switch (D5H) will be sent to the manufacturer to be X-rayed 
 
 • With the switch being X-rayed, we can pinpoint where exactly the switch failed. We believe the failure 
 
 point is either on the exposed wires or on the switches board itself. 
 
 • With the findings from the X-ray, we will devise a plan not to allow this to happen in the future.

Notes:
Engineer pumped down to plug depth and was able to read the switch, but was 
 
 unable to initiate the igniter due to a short. After multiple attempts it was 
 
 decided to pull out of hole and swap gun strings.
Description of Incident:
Engineer pumped down to plug depth and was able to read the switch, but was 
 
 unable to initiate the igniter due to a short. After multiple attempts it was 
 
 decided to pull out of hole and swap gun strings.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Upon investigation of the Xferno Igniter, it was found that the Igniter had been 
 
 crushed when installed into the firing head. This can be caused by excessive 
 
 torque when the Xferno retainer nut is installed or if debris is left in the Xferno 
 
 borehole in the firing head.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
There has been a design change to the XConnect Xferno firing head that will 
 
 mitigate this happening in the future. A lip has been added to the Xferno firing 
 
 head that will not allow the xferno retainer nut to seat far enough down to 
 
 crush the igniter. 
 
  
 
 New firing heads will be sent out with a lip not allowing the igniter to be 
 
 pressed in far enough to repeat this issue.

Notes:
At depth for stage 10 the engineer set the plug, shot gun 1, and gun 2. When trying to 
 
 inventory for gun 3 he had lost switches from gun 7 (57H) down. The engineer shot the 
 
 remaining guns
Well Name and #: David Beck USA 24-7H ,GT USA 11-18H ,Millie USA 21-18H,. Pugh USA 14-7TFH, Reems USA 14-7H, TR USA 24-7H, Maas USA 11-18TFH, Pavlish USA 21-18TFH ,Terrel USA 24-7TFH
Description of Incident:
At depth for stage 10 the engineer set the plug, shot gun 1, and gun 2. When trying to 
 
 inventory for gun 3 he had lost switches from gun 7 (57H) down. The engineer shot the 
 
 remaining guns
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
On surface it was found that gun 7 had gone off. A Ranger FQC was dispatched to location 
 
 and could not find anything wrong with the remaining gun string. The Control Fire data 
 
 shows this gun was never armed. 
 
  
 
 The FQC brought the stems from gun 7 and 8 to the XConnect shop. Both stems had 2 dots 
 
 on them. Likely making this the 3rd run. Metering stem 57H showed no indication of it being 
 
 bad. We then hooked it up and shot threw switch 57H to a switch below. When we did this 
 
 we had read ½ of the voltage sent to the switch below on the detonator lead of switch 57H. 
 
 We should not have had any voltage reading here as that switch ID was not armed. 
 
  
 
 Next, we melted stem 57H. We could not find anything visibly wrong with the switch or the 
 
 wires. 
 
  
 
 Lastly with the wires nicely laid out and separated we shot through a switch below switch 
 
 57H. Again, we had a voltage reading on the detonator lead of 57H when we should not 
 
 have
Root Cause/Conclusion: V2 switch 57H has an internal failure causing a voltage leak to the detonator lead.

Notes:

 After this event engineer swapped out the switch gun 8 instead of gun 9 and proceeded to

Description of Incident:
At 200’ and attempted to characterize gun string. Could only read BRT and top 2 guns, 
 
 Short after gun number 2 
 
  
 
 After this event engineer swapped out the switch gun 8 instead of gun 9 and proceeded to 
 
 run in hole. 
 
  
 
 The engineer shot plug and six guns before losing communication with guns below gun 9. 
 
 After adjusting the communication voltage, it was decided to shoot the remaining two guns 
 
 and come out of hole.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
On surface, it was found that the switch stem in gun 2 was intermittently shorted blue 
 
 downhole communication bullet terminal to ground. The switch stem was returned to 
 
 XConnect for further investigation. 
 
 • At XConnect the intermittent short was confirmed with the blasters meter. 
 
 • Stem was melted down to reveal internal wiring for any nicks. 
 
 • Once the stem was melted, it was observed that there were small abrasions on the 
 
 red wire where the blue wire bullet terminal rested. This is believed to be the point

Notes: Misfired Barrel
Well Name and #: Andrea Faye 15-22H, Kyle Donovan 15-22H, Kory Dale 10-3H, Pattie Ann 10-3H, Terry Lynn 10-3H
Description of Incident: Misfired Barrel
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
After receiving a picture of the misfire carrier, it is apparent that the detonator was not 
 
 installed into the detonator block. The detonator had to have been laying under the bottom 
 
 side of the block in order to dent the carrier and break up the detonator block on that side. 
 
 The top side of the detonator block should have been blown up by the detonator.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
The detonator was not installed correctly at the XConnect facility. The reprocussions of this 
 
 event and a follow up of our quality control process will be discussed with the XConnect 
 
 team and a direct discussion with JL. We will implement a dual signature into our process. 
 
 The person who loads the carrier and the person who does the final quality check will both 
 
 initial the carrier.

Investigation and Troubleshooting: The igniter was returned to XConnect for further investigation. It was found that the resistor inside of the Igniter burned but did not igniter powder inside of igniter. The low order was due to a significant loss of powder inside of igniter that resulted in a break in explosive train assembly. The cause of this loss of powder was the result of improper installation of an igniter in the firing head. The igniter was likely installed with a screwdriver instead of recommended nut driver. The screwdriver likely punctured blue rubber stopper on the bottom of igniter.
Root Cause/Conclusion: The crews will be retrained on the proper way to install the igniter.
