Notes: Stems after stage 2 return to surface melted.
Description of Incident: Stems after stage 2 return to surface melted.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: All stems confirmed having damage from exposure to temperatures around 350 degrees for an extended period. Signs included but were not limited to swelling, dimpling, and cracking. Two of the stems were poorly melted enough to look like they were exposed to a blast due to failed O-ring. It wasn’t reported that these subs were flooded, so this may not be the case. Lot dates on switch stems; June 2022 x 5, April 2022 x3, August 2021 x1, and two stems that lot date stamp was melted beyond recognition. With variation in lot dates returning to surface melted, there is unlikely a defect in the molding process.
Root Cause/Conclusion: If the issue continues, a fix that could be utilized in the future is to run Viton O-rings on the mini bridge bulkhead and top bulkhead. This would prevent the top bulkhead o-ring failure downhole, resulting in a Misfire.

Notes: Sea Snake stage 37 engineer saw a typical detonator break while shooting. Once on the surface, it was found that the top gun did not correctly fire. When the gun was opened, the detonator went in low order.
Description of Incident: Sea Snake stage 37 engineer saw a typical detonator break while shooting. Once on the surface, it was found that the top gun did not correctly fire. When the gun was opened, the detonator went in low order.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Upon further investigation of the low-ordered det, it was concluded that the most likely cause of the issue was either detonator was exposed to moisture or the det cord was not in the det block. The det was split and not fully detonated, which is consistent with what you would find with a wet det where the resistor blows but does not initiate the lead azide. The lack of damage to the det cord leaves the possibility open that the det cord was not in the det block. The bend in the det cord does look like it was in the det block, but I cannot say for sure that it was.
Root Cause/Conclusion: To mitigate either of the issues from occurring again, we have made two changes; we will be placing desiccant packs in every gun in the arming window and adding another QC check to the det cord. The desiccant packs will help absorb moisture in barrels after removing the top cap and protect the detonator. Quality Control employee will make a slash across det block and det cord to show that it has been checked instead of just performing a visual inspection. This will then be checked again by the employee installing the sub.

Notes: When the tandem sub was removed from the barrel, it would pull charge, which would pull the top bulkhead out of the end plate. This damaged two Lynx barrels. This did not cause any downtime or problems in the field.
Description of Incident: When the tandem sub was removed from the barrel, it would pull charge, which would pull the top bulkhead out of the end plate. This damaged two Lynx barrels. This did not cause any downtime or problems in the field.
Investigation and Troubleshooting: Upon further investigation, it was found that the coating used on the outside of G&H-made subs was more abrasive than subs manufactured by other vendors. This abrasive coating caused a tighter fit around subs and top bulkhead.
Root Cause/Conclusion: We will use a wire brush to remove this coating in future builds.

The company man decided to just shoot the topmost gun, POOH, and then pump some brine with acid to dissolve the plug. We shot the topmost gun and POOH. On surface, it was noticed that the top 2 guns fired, not just the topmost gun. It worked in our favor as now we could pump down another gun. The SQM found that the switch on the 2nd from the top gun had a short. We pumped down an 18 gunstring, shot it, and continued operations. NPT of 2.5 hrs.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
"Investigation & Troubleshooting: 
 
 When the stem was returned to XConnect, it was melted to expose internal wires. Once melted, it was confirmed that the blue wire (communication wire) was making intermittent contact with the green wire (hot wire). The knurled bullet connector on the blue wire was pressed against the green wire. After multiple uses, the blue bullet terminal was able to damage the green wire insulation to the point that it exposed conductor wire to green wire. This allowed voltage/current to ark from blue wire to green hot wire while the plug was being set. The voltage/current was significant enough to shoot gun number two while the plug switch was shot.
Root Cause/Conclusion: XConnect will adjust wire management accordingly. XConnect will also adjust the length of wires prior to the molding process to mitigate this event occurring again in the future."

Description of Incident: Stage 39 blue well. Shot plug and 6 guns and couldn't get gun 7 to shoot. Change communication settings, pump rate, pump down a few feet and came back up, reset computer and panel but no luck. Skip that gun and shot the rest. On surface all the wires and scotch locks look good nothing broken. The thing found was a very loose pin on the end plate. The deto metered good. The switch stem and carrier are set to the side.
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
"Investigation & Troubleshooting: 
 
 Once the gun string was on the surface detonator was checked through the endplate and 
 
 appropriately metered at 55 ohms. The switch stem was then metered all available QC 
 
 checks were performed, resulting in no abnormalities. The det and switch stem were 
 
 returned to XConnect for further investigation. The detonator was metered, confirming that 
 
 it metered adequately. The switch stem was checked with control fire software; the switch 
 
 was itemized and shot through correctly. There was no exterior damage observed on the 
 
 switch stem. The switch stem was then melted to expose internal wiring. While melting, it 
 
 was found that the green wire was broken from the bullet terminal. While testing the switch 
 
 stem in the shop, the green wire likely made enough contact to show voltage/contact being 
 
 passed through the switch. Downhole during misfire event contact was probably not made 
 
 between green wire and bullet terminal causing the open circuit. This is consistent with 
 
 findings when the switch stem is pried off the bottom endplate instead of pulled off straight."

Notes: Galvan Ranch C 434H, Stage 59 - unable to fire gun #5 of 10, reading open at the switch. Tried adjusting comm.'s, rate, and jarring the tool with no luck. As this is the final stage for this well and pad the WSM was adamant about skipping the gun and moving forward. Plug set 9/10 guns fired. will diagnose and post findings once on surface. @Adrian Sykes Sr. @Matt Bohn
Description of Incident: Galvan Ranch C 434H, Stage 59 - unable to fire gun #5 of 10, reading open at the switch. Tried adjusting comm.'s, rate, and jarring the tool with no luck. As this is the final stage for this well and pad the WSM was adamant about skipping the gun and moving forward. Plug set 9/10 guns fired. will diagnose and post findings once on surface. @Adrian Sykes Sr. @Matt Bohn
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
"Investigation & Troubleshooting: 
 
 On surface, det was metered, det meter properly. Scotch locks and wires were meters to ensure they were adequately depressed and that wires had continuity; meter checks were all correct. Stems were brought back to the XConnect shop for further investigation. The switch was shot through using a control fire software. There was no current or voltage when the switch was shot through; confirm what was seen in the field. The switch stem was melted down to expose internal wiring. The bullet terminal on the green wire broke at the base where the wire is crimped. There was damage to the bullet terminal’s open that is usually consistent with the sub being used to pry the switch off the bottom endplate. This could have been the cause of the bullet terminal breaking from the green wire. The failed stem was dated 2/9, so the damage could have occurred over time or months prior, with the stem ending up back in rotation.
Root Cause/Conclusion: XConnect rep and Ranger SQM went over the best practice with the crew and some things to look out for to mitigate this event from happening again. SQM and XConnect rep also threw away all the crew's old stems with an unknown number of runs. "

Notes:
"Stage 43 on 909h 
 
 Guns checked good at 200 and before pumping down down at marker joint. When got to plug depth i was only able to read pass the first gun. Got some rate to rattle the gun but kept saying open circuit after the first gun. I change the communication settings up and down but nothing work. I came up 300 ft and pump down again and still same result. Co man said to pooh at 150fpm and change gun strings. Will update findings. @Ronald Ray Sorrells @Tim Carlile @Adrian Sykes Sr. @Adrian Sykes Sr. @Ronald Ray Sorrells @jimbo @Rocky within the last two jobs we've seen an influx of communication issues that yield no root cause - on surface the gun will box and meter correctly; wiring will be in good order with solid scotch locking and no one is able to pinpoint what exactly went wrong. In communication with XConnect we've all loosely determined that these issues have been linked back to grounding and have tried to mitigate what we can in the field by a more thorough cleaning of the grounding point in the sub - which has still not solved the issue. 
 
  
 
 In my opinion these subs are past their life expectancy and out of tolerance. These are the same subs that have been out here since the beginning of this contract almost two years ago, and no one knows how long they were used prior to that. In talking to @austin ive been told the average lifespan per sub is around 200 runs, and at this point we have to be well over that mark. Before the start of the next job I would like to see what we can do about getting new subs, toleranced subs, or at least some kind of way to check the specs on the current subs we have out here. Please let me know if or what is possible to implement moving forward, thanks."

Investigation and Troubleshooting:
At the XConnect shop, the top bulkhead was metered, confirming an 
 
 intermittent connection. The exterior wire was inspected for any damage or 
 
 nicks, and none was seen. The top bulk was then cut in half to expose the 
 
 internal wire. With the inner wire exposed, the wire broke below where the 
 
 wire is crimped to the top bulkhead body. This wire was likely damaged 
 
 before being molded into the top bulkhead body. The manufacturer will be 
 
 made aware of and add this to QC checks. 
 
 "

Description of Incident:
"Event: Misrun 14815 
 
 4/3/2022 
 
 Start: 04:15 
 
 End: 06:35 
 
 Well: Devon Ren State 13-14D 431H 
 
 Stage: 37 
 
 Supervisor finished pumping down pulled up to plug depth and set plug. After shooting first gun could only read brt and guns 19&20. After resetting titan software and adjusting voltage and currents supervisor was able to regain all guns. After shooting gun #2 the same issue arose where he could only read brt and guns 19&20. Supervisor made call to FQC and tried pumping and jerking the toolstring as well as readjusting the voltage and current limits but were unsuccessful regaining tool string passed gun 19. Decision was made to shoot guns 19&20 for more holes and pull out of the hole and run in with new gun string. Once on surface tool string was broke off of brt and checked over. Gun string was checked with the verifier box and could not read passed gun 19. Gun string was broke apart between guns 19&18 and switch stem was pulled for investigation. There was nothing abnormal and could read through the carrier itself. Gun was sent in for further examination at the XConnect yard with both stems. 
 
 CREWS 
 
 Days: Tyler Hall, Kevin Kahrig, Miguel Duron 
 
 Nights: Jesus Torres, Anthony Lucarini, Cody Akers 
 
 Gun System: XConnect 
 
 FrNPT:0 
 
 WLNPT:2.3"
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
It was reported from the field that once on the surface tool string was checked with the Verifire box and could only read the top two guns (19&20). Gun string was then broken down, and connections were checked. Stems checked properly with blasters meter and verifire box once removed from the string. Pin connections were then checked for continuity, and no leaks were found with the blaster’s meter. Stems were sent to Xconnect for further investigation. 
 
  
 
 Titan control fire data was examined, to confirm a leak below gun 19. Gun check current limit was set to 125mA; software indicated that the current seen was 133mA. The WL engineer stated he changed current limits, but this was saved over on the next stage, so we could not confirm this. 
 
  
 
 At the Xconnect shop, the stems were once again metered and checked with control fire software. No issues were found with the blaster’s meter or control fire software. The top and bottom endplates were metered, confirming no leaks in these two components. The mini bridged bulkhead was removed from the bottom endplate to inspect for any metal debris in boreholes; none were seen. There were no markings on either switch stem, but both stems looked to be used prior. Stems were then melted to expose the internal wiring. With wires exposed, wires were inspected for nicks or damage, and no damage was observed. A meter was used to check each wire for any exposed conductor, none were found. It was noticed that the base of the bullet connector to the white wire on the stem from gun 19 was pressed against the black ground wire. This area was checked with a meter and did not indicate a leak occurring here, but during the process of melting, this arena could have been covered in melted plastic. Indicated by titan data, this was a minute leak, so it is also possible that there was fluid, metal shavings, or some other foreign substance on the bottom endplate pins. This foreign material may have been small enough not to be observed on initial troubleshooting or fell off when the switch was removed."

Notes: At 07:56 on 3/20/2022 while shooting stage 3 on the Ren State 13-14C 434H, supervisor was shooting gun #5 and titan software showed a potential misfire. During initial review of shot plot current and voltage both rise and current breaks sharply at .05A which is indicative of a fired det. Supervisor pumping on tool string and jerking on tool string to try to establish communication again but failed. Current and voltages limits were also adjusted yielding the same results. The decision was made to skip the gun and finish stage. FQC was dispatched. Once back on surface it was confirmed that gun 5 had not shot and gun was taken off the spent string and sat to the side for FQC investigation. Once FQC arrived the gun was opened up and inspected. Investigation showed that the detonator could not be read across the bulk head pin and ground. Once det was secured in det tube and metered it was showing to be an open in the detonator. Upon closer look the fluid/pressure disabling window of the A-140 had a hole in it suggesting the resistor in the A-140 broke during the shooting sequence.

Notes: Stage 29 of the 702H well, we set the plug. Went to shoot gun 1 and we could not read the past gun 7. Current was reading too high. After trying to change voltages and tolerances without success, we shot the remaining 9 guns. We will go in with another string to shoot the remaining 6 guns
Description of Incident: Stage 29 of the 702H well, we set the plug. Went to shoot gun 1 and we could not read the past gun 7. Current was reading too high. After trying to change voltages and tolerances without success, we shot the remaining 9 guns. We will go in with another string to shoot the remaining 6 guns
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
On surface, it was found that the switch stem in gun number seven would not properly meter 
 
 with VeriFire box or blasters meter. The switch was returned to XConnect for further 
 
 investigation. Markings on the switch stem indicated that this was the third run on the stem. 
 
 XConnect confirmed that the switch stem did, in fact, not meter correctly. It was found that 
 
 the diode could be read through the ground pins; this indicates that the blue wire was 
 
 contacting the black ground wire. The switch stem was then melted to determine why contact 
 
 was made between blue and ground wires. After the stem was melted, it was revealed that 
 
 the black wire had a nick where the blue bullet connector base would sit.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
XConnect will look into proper wire management during the molding process to ensure that 
 
 this does not occur again in the future. XConnect also does recommend that switch stems be 
 
 removed by hand by pulling straight out, this helps to ensure that bullet connectors don’t see 
 
 added stress during disassembly. "

Notes:
"Investigation & Troubleshooting: 
 
 When the stem was returned to XConnect, it was melted to expose internal wires. Once melted, it was confirmed that the blue wire (communication wire) was making intermittent contact with the green wire (hot wire). The knurled bullet connector on the blue wire was pressed against the green wire. After multiple uses, the blue bullet terminal was able to damage the green wire insulation to the point that it exposed conductor wire to green wire. This allowed voltage/current to ark from blue wire to green hot wire while the plug was being set. The voltage/current was significant enough to shoot gun number two while the plug switch was shot. 
 
 Corrective Measures: 
 
 XConnect will adjust wire management accordingly. XConnect will also adjust the length of wires prior to the molding process to mitigate this event occurring again in the future."

Notes: Stage 8 on 322H WL engineer checked guns at 200ft, and titan control fire panel prompted a “possible short circuit” warning. Multiple attempts were made to regain communication with the bottom four guns, resulting in a “possible short circuit” warning. It was decided to come off the well and swap the tool string."
Description of Incident: Stage 8 on 322H WL engineer checked guns at 200ft, and titan control fire panel prompted a “possible short circuit” warning. Multiple attempts were made to regain communication with the bottom four guns, resulting in a “possible short circuit” warning. It was decided to come off the well and swap the tool string."
Investigation and Troubleshooting: All components in question were troubleshot in the field, Switch stems were appropriately tested, and meter checks on the charge tube were checked adequately. With no failed components found in the field, parts were returned to XConnect for further investigation. At the XConnect facility, switch stems were checked with titan control fire panel, the board adequately inventoried all switches. Switch stems were then melted to expose internal wiring. All wires were free of nicks and any damage resulting in failures down the hole. Bottom endplates with the remaining length of wire were checked with a meter; no indication of the leak was found on the bottom endplate. The bridged mini bulkhead was removed from the bottom endplate to check for any metal debris; no foreign metals were found in the bridged mini bulkhead or bottom endplate. Top endplates were then metered, all metered properly. Top bulkheads were then removed from the top endplate. The top endplate of gun number three revealed having a substantial amount of residual motor oil that migrated around the protective boot. Motor oil is non-conductive, so this would not in and of itself lead to a short downhole. The substantial motor oil likely inhibited the protective boot from sealing around top bulkhead wiring. This allowed residual moisture from recently washed sub above to migrate to top bulkhead wiring, causing a leak at 200ft.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
Corrective Measures: 
 
  XConnects policy is to clean top endplates of oil with parts washer and air hose. This likely wasn’t washed well enough in the process. XConnect has also moved to a new style of top bulkhead that does not use a protective boot but instead utilizes the fully sealed body. This will eliminate the chances of this happening in the future."

Investigation and Troubleshooting:
Gun 7 was returned to surface and disassembled. Each component was checked for 
 
 mechanical and electrical integrity during disassembly. The detonator wires at the 
 
 top of gun 7 failed electrical checks. An open circuit was observed through the 
 
 detonator where continuity is required. As a result, the detonator did not perform 
 
 as designed. Prior to running in the well, all checks were completed as per section 
 
 5.1 of XConnect SOP.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
Stopped work to review gun assembly process and discuss verification of work 
 
 quality by second crew member. Also discussed need for increased attention to 
 
 detail and not getting in a hurry. 
 
 Redistribute XConnect SOP to all crews with specific emphasis on electrical and 
 
 mechanical checks in section 5.1.

Description of Incident: 33H stage 23 set plug, shot 3 guns, and lost communication below gun 9. Possible gun short. Contacted Tim and Matt adjusted voltage, pulled on it and slacked off with no luck. Devon did not want to roll rate and instead we skipped the 5 guns and adjusted for offsets. Will be RIH with a 5 barrel gun string
Investigation and Troubleshooting:
n the field switch stem, 8 and 9 were tested, both switch stems were tested correctly. Gun 
 
 8/9 switch stems and top/bottom endplates were returned to XConnect for further 
 
 investigation. Stems and endplates were checked with a hunting titan panel. All tests 
 
 resulted in a proper inventory of the two switch stems. Switch stem 8 and 9 were then 
 
 melted to expose wires for further investigation. All wires were free of nicks or any damage 
 
 that would compromise the switch stem. After it was concluded that the switch stems were 
 
 not the issue, further inspection of the bottom endplate took place. Bottom endplate 
 
 checked as correctly with blasters meter. Bridged Mini bulkhead was removed from the 
 
 bottom end; it was then noticed that there was a small amount of exposed metal near the 
 
 banana plug of the white through wire. This exposed metal allowed through wire to gun 
 
 number 8 to short out; the exposed metal was so small that it likely didn’t short out until the 
 
 bridge shifted while shooting the three previous guns. Corrective Measures: 
 
 Manufacture of the bridged mini bulkhead will be made aware of the error and instructed to 
 
 add this QC check to part of their process. XConnect will also add to their inspection of 
 
 bridged mini bulkheads before being pressed into the bottom endplate.
Root Cause/Conclusion:
XConnect employees have been made aware of manufacturer defects and will add this to 
 
 the QC checks of the bridged mini bulkhead."

Notes:
Upon shooting gun 8 of 15 it read open. The system was reset and I 
 
 rolled back into it with the same outcome. The remaining guns 
 
 were shot and the Well Site Manager is calling this a stage. Upon 
 
 investigation, it was found that 
 
 Investigation & Troubleshooting: 
 
 All internal components were present and correct. Found that the 
 
 detonator did not read through to the end plate of the carrier. The 
 
 RED wire from the detonator to ground was reading +12 ohm when 
 
 metering to the carrier. From the wire lug to the carrier was nearly 
 
 the same. Lug to wire end read one. Manipulated the wire at the 
 
 allen screw and got some variance and intermittent open. Checked 
 
 tightness on the allen screw and it seemed tight, the lug wasn’t 
 
 freely moving either. Was able tighten it more and got it down to 4 
 
 Ohms, still high. Took off the wire and it read =/- 1 Ohm. There was 
 
 a kinked spot and some damage to the coating. This did not affect 
 
 the meter readings and would not have caused an open if shorted 
 
 to the ground since it is the ground. The allen screws do have a 
 
 moly coating on them. Cause found is bad detonator ground. 
 
 Corrective Measures: 
 
 Double check the tightness on all of the carrier allen screws and 
 
 the ground screw. Make sure to meter this prior to building the gun 
 
 string."

Notes:
After setting plug and shooting 8 guns. The gun string showed potential short 
 
 after gun 11. Lowered communication voltage and raised delta currents. 
 
 Still showed a potential leak. Client decided to skip guns 9,10 and shoot the 
 
 remaining guns. Called it a stage. 
 
 Investigation & Troubleshooting: On surface took the shot gun 11 
 
 above off and checked gun 10 with the verifier. Verifier resulted with two 
 
 switches. Metered switch with .493 v and good conductivity to ground through 
 
 switch. All scotch locks were connected properly, and there were no skinned 
 
 wires. Everything checked good in gun 9 as well. With gun 11 being shot it is 
 
 inconclusive on what the issue was"

Notes:
After setting the plug and shooting 11 
 
 guns, software showed an open circuit below gun 17. Decision was 
 
 made to POOH and run another string in to complete the stage. 
 
 While POOH, several switch inventories were performed, with 
 
 different numbers of switches read each time, until finally all 
 
 switches would inventory again. 
 
 Investigation & Troubleshooting: Arrived on location and tested 
 
 the remaining guns with the surface tester and all switches verified. 
 
 Began breaking guns down and found a bad, flattened banana pin 
 
 at gun 17 that was flat enough that when the switch was installed, 
 
 it could wiggle side to side quite easily. It is believed that this issue 
 
 caused the intermittent issues."
